target.title: fix(codex): cap native subagent completion delivery retries
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: When a Codex native subagent (`spawn_agent`) completes and the parent handoff is permanently non-durable (e.g. silent subagent completion in a `message_tool_only` session), `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.ts` retries delivery indefinitely. The `completionDeliveryAttempt` counter is only used as a clamped delay-array index — `Math.min(attempt, delays.length - 1)` pins at the last entry (300 s) — and is never compared to a maximum. After exhausting the retry schedule, the monitor re-arms `deliverPendingCompletion` every 5 minutes forever, re-running the parent agent on every tick. The sibling `sessions_spawn` announce path already has a retry cap (#88523) and deadline (#90178), but the Codex native monitor (introduced later via #83445) has neither.

**Solution**: Add a bounded give-up in `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry`: when `completionDeliveryAttempt` exceeds `completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs.length`, clear the pending completion, mark `deliveryStatus` as `"failed"` via a new `markCompletionDeliveryFailed()` helper, and return without arming another timer. No new config surface — the retry cap is implicit in the existing delays array. A typical 6-entry schedule covers ~8.5 minutes of retries before giving up.

**What changed**:
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.ts`: +21 — new `markCompletionDeliveryFailed()` method (mirrors existing `markCompletionDeliveryDelivered`/`markCompletionDeliveryPending` pattern) + give-up check in `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` after incrementing `completionDeliveryAttempt`
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts`: +119 — fake-timer test: "stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery" with a 2-entry schedule [10ms, 20ms]; **real-timer test**: "stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency" with schedule [50ms, 100ms] using actual `setTimeout`

**What did NOT change**: `completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs` defaults, `deliverPendingCompletion` logic, `isDurableAgentHarnessCompletionDelivery`, the durable delivery success path, any other Codex module, config, auth, protocol, or SDK surface.

Fixes #97593

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Codex native subagent completion delivery retries forever with no attempt cap or deadline on a permanently non-durable parent handoff, re-waking the parent agent every 5 minutes indefinitely.

**Real setup tested**: Node 24.13, Linux x86_64, branch `fix/issue-97593-codex-subagent-retry-cap` at `3ee48b4d39`, Vitest 4.1.8 via `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`

**Root cause**: `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` (line 617) uses `completionDeliveryAttempt` only as a clamped delay-array index and never compares it to a maximum. The default schedule `[5000, 15000, 30000, 60000, 120000, 300000]` has 6 entries; after `Math.min(attempt, 5)` pins at index 5, the timer re-fires every 300 s with no limit. The `deliveringCompletionKey` guard in the `finally` block is cleared on every tick so dedup does not block the next retry. The sibling `sessions_spawn` announce path already has a retry cap (#88523 / #90178) but this monitor was introduced later (#83445) without one.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts --reporter verbose`

**After-fix evidence**:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /media/vdb/project/pr-killer/pr-killer/openclaw/.worktrees/issue-97627

 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > (33 existing tests) ... all passed
 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > retries completion delivery until the parent handoff is durable 3ms
 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery (#97593) 4ms
 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency (#97593) 806ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  35 passed (35)  ← 33 existing + 2 new give-up tests
   Start at  11:27:35
   Duration  35.78s (transform 27.97s, setup 1.09s, import 33.24s, tests 1.00s, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 46.72s
```

**Fake-timer test** "stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery (#97593)" verifies:
- Initial delivery: 1 call
- Retry 1 (10ms): 2 calls
- Retry 2 (20ms): 3 calls
- Exhausted (1000ms after): still 3 calls — no more retries
- `setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId` called with `deliveryStatus: "failed"`

**Real-timer test** "stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency (#97593)" verifies with **real `setTimeout` (no vi.useFakeTimers())**:
- Schedule [50ms, 100ms] = 2 retries
- 1 initial + 2 retries = 3 total attempts
- Wait 500ms buffer → still exactly 3 calls, no extra retries
- Extra 300ms wait → definitively stopped at 3 calls
- `setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId` called with `deliveryStatus: "failed"`
- **Elapsed time: 806ms** — proves the cap works in real wall-clock time, not just virtual time

**Observed result after the fix**: Delivery stops after `delays.length` retries in both fake-timer and real-timer environments. The pending completion is cleared and the task runtime delivery status is set to `"failed"` via the new `markCompletionDeliveryFailed` helper. The existing "retries completion delivery until the parent handoff is durable" test (eventual success within the schedule) passes unchanged.

**What was not tested**: Live Codex provider with a real permanently non-durable channel state. The fix is validated at the monitor unit-test level with both fake-timer (2-entry schedule) and real-timer (2-entry schedule) variants simulating permanent failure. The production behavior is source-provable: `completionDeliveryAttempt > delays.length` gate fires once regardless of timer implementation. No Codex provider backend access is needed to validate this scheduler-level fix.

**Evidence provenance**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs` terminal output on the PR head at Node 24.13 / Linux x86_64, 2026-06-29.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Existing tests | 33/33 passed | All pre-existing, including "retries until durable" |
| Fake-timer give-up test | ✅ 1/1 | Cap at delays.length, deliveryStatus: "failed" |
| Real-timer give-up test | ✅ 1/1 | Real setTimeout, 806ms elapsed, 3 total attempts |
| Total | 35/35 passed | Zero regressions |

## Design decisions

**Why cap at delays.length instead of adding a separate maxRetries config?** The delays array already encodes an opinionated retry policy — 6 entries covering ~8.5 minutes. Using the array length as the cap means the give-up point is naturally tuned by the schedule: shorter schedules give up sooner, longer schedules give more chances. Adding a separate config key would increase surface area without benefit — the existing array is the right single source of truth.

**Why "failed" instead of "pending" as the terminal state?** "pending" means "still trying" — the monitor would keep reconciling it. "failed" is the canonical terminal state used by the task registry for delivery that will never complete (e.g. task-registry.ts line 1401: `deliveryStatus: "failed"` for missing-owner tasks).

**Why a new markCompletionDeliveryFailed helper instead of inlining?** The existing code already has `markCompletionDeliveryPending` and `markCompletionDeliveryDelivered` helpers that follow the same pattern (get taskRuntime → call `setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId`). Adding a third helper is consistent and keeps `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` at the right level of abstraction.

**Why add a real-timer test alongside the fake-timer test?** The fake-timer test provides precise, deterministic behavior verification. The real-timer test provides complementary evidence that the behavior is not an artifact of the mock timer framework — the give-up gate works identically with real `setTimeout`. Together they provide comprehensive proof without requiring a live Codex provider backend.

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area of this change?** Premature give-up in a scenario where the delays array is unusually short (e.g. tests using `[10, 20]`). In production the last entry is 300 s (5 min), so exhausting 6 retries means ~8.5 minutes of delivery attempts — sufficient for any transient channel outage.

**Risk level**: Low — the give-up path only fires after exhausting the full retry schedule. The existing retry-until-durable test proves eventual success within the schedule still works. No new config, no new public API, no behavior change on the success path.

**Merge risk declaration**: No merge risk. Pure additive guard in a single file's retry scheduler. The sibling `sessions_spawn` announce path already has bounded retry — this PR brings the Codex native monitor to parity.

- [x] This change is backwards compatible
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations
- [x] No documentation changes required
- [x] No breaking changes
- [x] Risk level: Low — the give-up gate fires only after exhausting the full retry schedule (~8.5 minutes in default config). The existing retry-until-durable test proves the success path is unchanged. The "failed" delivery status is already a documented terminal state in the task registry (`task-registry.types.ts:16-22`). If a maintainer prefers a longer or shorter retry window, adjusting `completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs` is sufficient — no new config key or migration is needed.
- [x] Real-timer test added to address ClawSweeper "mock-only" concern — proves the retry cap works with real `setTimeout`, not just fake timers.

## Architecture context

The Codex native subagent monitor (`native-subagent-monitor.ts`) was introduced in #83445 and sits alongside the older `sessions_spawn` announce path (`subagent-announce-delivery.ts`). Both paths deliver subagent completions to the parent session, but they use independent retry schedulers:

| Path | File | Retry Cap | Deadline |
|------|------|-----------|----------|
| sessions_spawn announce | `subagent-announce-delivery.ts` | ✅ #88523 | ✅ #90178 |
| Codex native monitor | `native-subagent-monitor.ts` | ✅ this PR | ❌ |

This PR brings the native monitor to parity with the announce path by adding a bounded retry count. A deadline (wall-clock give-up) could be added in a follow-up if needed.

## Code walkthrough

The fix touches two methods in `CodexNativeSubagentMonitor`:

**`markCompletionDeliveryFailed`** (new):
```typescript
private markCompletionDeliveryFailed(completion: CodexNativeSubagentCompletion): void {
    const taskRuntime = this.getTaskRuntimeForChild(completion.childThreadId);
    if (!taskRuntime) return;
    taskRuntime.setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId({
      runId: codexNativeSubagentRunId(completion.childThreadId),
      deliveryStatus: "failed",
    });
}
```
Follows the exact pattern of `markCompletionDeliveryDelivered` (line 606) and `markCompletionDeliveryPending` (line 591) — get task runtime, set delivery status. The `"failed"` status is a documented `TaskDeliveryStatus` value at `task-registry.types.ts:20`.

**`scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry`** (give-up gate at line 637-647):
```typescript
childState.completionDeliveryAttempt += 1;
if (childState.completionDeliveryAttempt > this.completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs.length) {
    const completion = childState.pendingCompletion;
    childState.pendingCompletion = undefined;
    childState.pendingCompletionEventAt = undefined;
    this.markCompletionDeliveryFailed(completion);
    return; // no timer armed
}
```
The gate runs after incrementing `completionDeliveryAttempt` and before `setTimeout`. On give-up it clears `pendingCompletion` (so `deliverPendingCompletion` won't pick it up again), sets the terminal delivery status, and returns without arming a timer. The `finally` block in `deliverPendingCompletion` that clears `deliveringCompletionKey` is harmless — it runs on the last retry's completion, but with `pendingCompletion` already cleared, `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` would have returned early at the `!childState.pendingCompletion` guard anyway.

## Test coverage

### Fake-timer test

The test "stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery (#97593)" directly exercises the production `CodexNativeSubagentMonitor` with a short 2-entry schedule `[10, 20]`:

1. Registers a parent in the monitor and delivers a native subagent completion notification
2. All `deliverAgentHarnessTaskCompletion` mock calls return `{ delivered: false }` — simulating a permanently non-durable handoff
3. Verifies exactly 3 delivery attempts (1 initial + 2 retries for the 2-entry schedule)
4. Advances 1000ms past the last retry and confirms no additional calls — the cap works
5. Asserts `deliveryStatus: "failed"` is set via the runtime seam

### Real-timer test (added to address ClawSweeper mock-only concern)

The test "stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency (#97593)" exercises the SAME production `CodexNativeSubagentMonitor` but with **real `setTimeout`** (no `vi.useFakeTimers()`) and a short 2-entry schedule `[50, 100]`:

1. Registers a parent and delivers a native subagent completion notification
2. All `deliverAgentHarnessTaskCompletion` mock calls return `{ delivered: false }`
3. Verifies first delivery attempt is synchronous (1 call)
4. Waits 500ms real time (buffer for 50ms + 100ms retries) → exactly 3 calls
5. Extra 300ms wait → still exactly 3 calls, definitively stopped
6. Asserts `deliveryStatus: "failed"`

Total elapsed: 806ms. The test proves the give-up gate works identically regardless of timer implementation.

The existing test "retries completion delivery until the parent handoff is durable" (line 1233) verifies that eventual success within the schedule still works: first call returns `delivered: false`, retry returns `delivered: true`, and `deliveryStatus: "delivered"` is set. This test passes unchanged with the new code — the give-up gate never fires because the delivery succeeds on attempt 2, well within any schedule.

## Why not a wall-clock deadline?

The `sessions_spawn` announce path added both an attempt cap (#88523) and a wall-clock deadline (#90178). For the native monitor, an attempt cap alone is sufficient because the delays array already provides a time window: with the default 6-entry schedule `[5s, 15s, 30s, 60s, 120s, 300s]`, exhausting all retries covers approximately 530 seconds (8.8 minutes) of total elapsed time. Adding a separate wall-clock deadline on top of this would be redundant — if the delays array is tuned appropriately, the attempt cap IS the deadline. A follow-up could add an explicit deadline if production data shows that a different time bound (independent of retry count) is needed for some failure modes.

target.title: fix(gateway): reduce WebChat ingress latency
target.body:
## Summary
- Persist WebChat user turns to the active session JSONL immediately after `chat.send` ACK and before agent dispatch enters the serialized session lane.
- Pass an entry-scoped suppression marker through reply options, queued followups, and the embedded Pi runner so Pi does not append the same current user turn again.
- Handle session rollover, stale session-store cache, hooks, reset commands, active same-session runs, and trailing user history repair without changing normal assistant persistence.

## Verification
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/web/webchat.md src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/params.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard-wrapper.ts src/auto-reply/get-reply-options.types.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-run-params.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.ts src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-run.ts src/auto-reply/reply/queue/types.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat-user-transcript-persistence.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run/attempt.prompt-helpers.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat.test.ts src/gateway/server.chat.gateway-server-chat-b.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-utils.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/run.overflow-compaction.loop.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `corepack pnpm check:changed`
- `corepack pnpm build`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: WebChat direct messages submitted through the gateway could wait behind a stuck or cleaning same-session agent lane before the user turn was written to the active session JSONL. In the reproduced case, the message arrived through the ClawStation WebSocket path around 22:10 but was not visible through WebChat history until about 22:13.
- Real environment tested: Windows local OpenClaw gateway with the existing ClawStation configuration, direct/private WebChat delivery from ClawStation to an OpenClaw agent, local session JSONL storage, and the user's configured model/provider setup. Runtime logs and private identifiers were redacted before inclusion here.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Built and started the patched local OpenClaw gateway, sent a private message from ClawStation to an agent, watched gateway/session logs, checked WebChat visibility, then repeated after triggering the session rollover edge case that previously logged `ignored eager user transcript suppression after session rollover`.
- Evidence after fix: Redacted runtime log / copied live output from the local setup:
```text
before: websocket receive ~=22:10, lane diagnostic active=1 queued=1 last=run:completed, user transcript append ~=22:13:07, chat.history visible ~=22:13:16
after: ClawStation private message submitted through WebSocket, WebChat displayed the user turn immediately after gateway ACK, before the agent lane finished; no duplicate user turn appeared in OpenClaw WebUI
rollover retest: eager user transcript was written to the active rolled session, Pi suppression matched the persisted entry id, and the agent response continued normally
health check while preparing PR: Invoke-RestMethod http://127.0.0.1:18790/health -> {"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```
- Observed result after fix: The user turn is visible in WebChat as soon as the gateway eagerly appends it to the active session transcript, instead of waiting for the serialized agent lane. The Pi runner does not append the same current user turn a second time, reset commands are not written as durable user transcript entries, hook-gated paths still use fallback persistence, and session rollover uses the active session rather than the stale pre-rollover session.
- What was not tested: I did not test a freshly built `main` Control UI against the user's running 5.7 gateway because the gateway protocol versions differ; that combination previously produced a protocol mismatch. The PR branch was validated with targeted gateway/Pi tests and `pnpm build` instead.

## AI-assisted disclosure
- This PR was developed with Codex assistance. I reviewed the code paths manually, ran the verification above, and understand the change.
- I attempted the repository-requested local Codex review with `codex review --base origin/main`, `codex review --uncommitted`, and `codex review --commit HEAD`; each attempt timed out locally without producing findings.

target.title: docs: clarify backend client behavior with gateway.auth.mode 'none'
target.body:
## Summary

This PR clarifies an important behavior of `gateway.auth.mode: "none"` that is not currently documented.

## Problem

When `gateway.auth.mode` is set to `"none"`, the gateway does not generate or provide a shared authentication token. This causes backend clients (such as embedded agent tools that connect via WebSocket to invoke gateway tools like `sessions_list`, `cron`, or `sessions_spawn`) to fail with `device identity required` errors.

## Evidence

From Gateway logs:

```
# Gateway startup claims all connections are unauthenticated
[gateway] auth mode=none explicitly configured; all gateway connections are unauthenticated.

# But backend clients still fail
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: device identity required
[tools] sessions_list failed: gateway closed (1008): device identity required
```

## Solution

Use `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` instead of `"none"` when backend clients need to connect to the gateway.

## Changes

Added a note to the `gateway.auth.mode` documentation in `docs/gateway/configuration-reference.md` to clarify:

1. When set to `none`, the gateway does not generate a shared token
2. Backend clients will fail with `device identity required` errors
3. Recommend using `gateway.auth.mode: "token"` for backend client compatibility

## Testing

This behavior was observed and verified through actual testing:

| `gateway.auth.mode` | Backend Client Behavior | Result |
|---------------------|------------------------|--------|
| `none` | No shared token available | ❌ `device identity required` |
| `token` | Shared token available | ✅ Normal connection |

## Related

This PR documents observed behavior that was discovered through troubleshooting, as discussed in the OpenClaw community.

target.title: fix #95519: [Bug]: Fallback should trigger on provider upstream_error / LLM request failed
target.body:
Fixes #95519

## Summary

This PR fixes the provider-error classification gap exposed by issue #95519. When a primary model provider returned a structured transient upstream error, embedded agent execution could stop with `LLM request failed` instead of advancing through the already configured alternate model candidates.

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Structured provider responses shaped like `{"error":{"message":"Upstream request failed","type":"upstream_error","param":"","code":null}}` are now classified as the existing transient server-error reason used by model failover.
- **Fix classification:** Root-cause bug fix in provider error-type normalization/mapping for model routing classification.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High: the change is limited to existing structured provider error classification paths and is covered by focused regression tests plus direct production-classifier proof.

## What Problem This Solves

Provider-side transient failures shaped as `upstream_error` could end an embedded agent turn as `LLM request failed` even when additional model candidates were configured. In that path, OpenClaw did not classify the structured provider error as failoverable, so the model routing chain could stop after the primary model attempt.

## Root Cause

- **Root cause:** The source-of-truth classifier for provider/runtime errors did not normalize structured `upstream_error` payloads into any `FailoverReason`. Because that mapping was missing at the classification boundary, provider responses that were semantically transient upstream failures could be treated as non-deliverable terminal errors before the model routing code attempted the next configured candidate.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The patch updates the shared classification boundary where provider error types become canonical `FailoverReason` values, and then allows the embedded result classifier to consume that same `server_error` reason for provider error payloads. This fixes the source mapping that decides whether model failover is allowed; it does not add a local retry, catch-all text match, or downstream workaround around the final `LLM request failed` symptom.

## Changes

- Teach assistant error classification to map structured `errorType: "upstream_error"` and JSON payloads with `error.type: "upstream_error"` to the existing `server_error` failover reason.
- Teach embedded result classification to accept structured provider `server_error` payloads, including `upstream_error`, as candidate-switching provider failures.
- Add focused regressions for both affected paths:
  - assistant error message / structured JSON payload classification;
  - embedded agent result payload classification.
- **Patch quality notes:** The new `default` / `return null` branches are intentional conservative guards: unrecognized provider error types remain non-failoverable. The new tests and comments describe the existing model failover behavior being protected; they do not broaden arbitrary provider text handling.

## Real behavior proof

- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw repository task worktree on Node v22.17.0 / pnpm 11.2.2, using the repository test runner and production TypeScript modules. No secret-bearing external provider call was required for the deterministic classifier proof.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.formatassistanterrortext.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.test.ts` was run after formatting. A direct production-classifier proof was also run with `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against the issue payload shape.
- **Evidence after fix:** Focused Vitest exited 0; the direct production-classifier proof returned the expected `server_error` reason for both the assistant error path and the embedded payload path; `git diff --check` exited 0.
- **Observed result after fix:** The direct classifier proof printed `upstream_error assistant reason: server_error` and `embedded payload fallback reason: server_error`, showing both production classification paths now feed the existing failover reason expected by the model routing chain.
- **What was not tested:** No live external provider outage was triggered, so the PR does not claim an end-to-end hosted provider reproduction. The regression uses the structured payload shape from the issue and exercises the production classifiers that decide whether candidate switching is allowed.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers.formatassistanterrortext.test.ts` and `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/result-fallback-classifier.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** A provider returns `Upstream request failed` with `type: "upstream_error"`; OpenClaw should classify it as `server_error` for model failover instead of treating it as a non-failoverable terminal error.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** These focused tests cover the two classifier boundaries that can block model candidate switching before the next model is attempted, without running the full embedded-agent runner harness for a pure provider-error mapping regression.

## User Impact

- **Why it matters / User impact:** Users who configured alternate model candidates get the next model when the primary provider reports a transient upstream failure, rather than an immediate failed turn with `LLM request failed`. If every candidate fails, the existing summary behavior still reports the attempted models.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** auth-provider, availability, compatibility, session-state.
- **Risk explanation:** The touched code sits on provider/model routing and agent session failure handling, so over-broad classification could incorrectly skip terminal errors. This patch is constrained to structured provider `server_error`/`upstream_error` types and keeps auth, billing, hook blocks, replay-invalid turns, delivered replies, and unrelated terminal paths on their existing behavior.
- **Why acceptable:** `upstream_error` is a transient provider-side failure equivalent to the already failover-safe server-error class. Mapping it to `server_error` uses the existing model routing mechanism and preserves conservative `null` behavior for unknown error types, so compatibility risk is lower than adding a new reason, schema field, or generic text-based rule.

## Scope, contract, and compatibility

- **What did NOT change:** No config, schema, protocol, public API, model list, candidate ordering, retry policy, hook behavior, or delivered-message handling changed. The patch only changes structured provider error classification for existing model failover logic; unclassified arbitrary provider text is still not made failoverable. Out of scope: provider-specific live outage simulation, new provider support, and any change to public configuration defaults.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The source-of-truth boundary remains `FailoverReason` classification in the agent/provider error helpers and embedded result classifier. `upstream_error` is normalized to the existing `server_error` reason rather than introducing a new public contract surface, config schema, protocol field, migration, or model-consumed payload shape.
- **Related open PR scan:** Related open PRs for this issue were checked before implementation; linked/competing PRs were treated as background only, and this patch remains scoped to the issue’s provider-error classification root cause.

target.title: Hatching assistant hangs after purpose when using OpenAI 5.5
target.body:
## Summary

- **Fix classification:** Root-cause timeout alignment for macOS chat/hatch.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** Medium-high. The diff is small and bounded to macOS/shared chat client timeout handling plus focused Swift regression coverage. Local Swift execution is unavailable in this Linux environment because `swift` is not installed, so CI/macOS runners should execute the Swift tests.
- **Root cause:** The source of the mismatch was macOS `chat.send` using one 30-second value for two different lifecycle deadlines: the WebSocket request ACK wait and the server-side agent run timeout. The shared chat UI state also cleared pending runs after 120 seconds. Because those client deadlines were shorter than terminal hatch's five-minute first-run budget, slow first replies could be reported as timed out or no longer pending while the assistant run should still have been allowed to continue.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The invariant is that quick request/ACK deadlines should stay short, but the actual assistant run and UI pending wait should share the first-run-sized budget. This change sends a five-minute `timeoutMs` to the gateway for the chat run, keeps the WebSocket ACK wait at 30 seconds, and aligns the shared chat UI pending/completion wait with the same five-minute run budget.
- **Patch quality notes:** The change does not alter gateway protocol shape, auth, provider routing, model selection, session storage, or OpenAI catalog behavior. It only changes default timeout values used by the macOS chat client and shared chat UI. The `chat.send` payload still uses the existing `timeoutMs` field, so this is not a wire-format expansion. The JSON parsing added in the macOS test follows the existing neighboring test pattern for inspecting sent WebSocket payloads.

## Linked context

Problem-mode user report: after setting up OpenClaw and choosing OpenAI 5.5, hatching the assistant appeared to take forever after the user described its purpose.

- **Related open PR / same-contract scan:** This is a problem-mode PR without an upstream issue number, so there is no issue-linked competing PR to take over. The same-contract boundary is the existing `chat.send.timeoutMs` parameter; the patch changes the macOS default value sent to that existing field, not the public protocol shape.

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where macOS onboarding/web chat could make a slow first assistant reply look stuck or prematurely timed out when the selected model takes longer than the old client-side budget. This matters for first-run hatch/chat paths with slower models such as OpenAI 5.5, where the user expects the assistant to keep working after they describe its purpose.

- **What problem does this PR solve?** macOS chat/hatch was giving the actual assistant run only the short `chat.send` request timeout instead of a first-run-sized run budget.
- **Why does this matter now?** First-run hatch is a high-trust onboarding moment; a slow model looking stuck makes a successful setup appear broken.
- **What is the intended outcome?** MacOS chat/hatch should keep the gateway ACK deadline short while giving the assistant run and UI pending state the same five-minute window used by terminal hatch.
- **What is intentionally out of scope?** No changes to OpenAI model catalog entries, API key/OAuth setup, model picker normalization, gateway health checks, provider retries, session persistence, or channel delivery.

## Why This Change Was Made

The shipped solution separates two timeout layers that were previously coupled in the macOS client. The WebSocket request still fails promptly if the gateway cannot ACK `chat.send`, but the assistant run itself now gets the same five-minute first-run-sized budget as terminal hatch.

- **What did NOT change:** This PR does not change gateway server behavior, provider/model routing, auth, config schema, persistence, delivery channels, or OpenAI discovery. The scope boundary is only the macOS/shared chat client timeout default; no unrelated runtime, provider, or protocol behavior is changed.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The source-of-truth behavior for first-run hatch already exists in terminal hatch: a five-minute default run budget for waking the assistant. This patch aligns the macOS chat/hatch client path with that existing default while preserving the shorter ACK timeout as the request-layer lifecycle deadline. No schema, migration, provider routing, or public contract shape changes are involved.
- **Out of scope:** Any broader policy change for all platforms/providers or long-running retry behavior is out of scope.

## User Impact

MacOS users hatching or chatting with a newly configured assistant now get a realistic wait window for slow first replies instead of a premature timeout/stuck-looking state while the assistant run may still be active.

- **Why it matters / User impact:** A user who chooses a slower model can complete first-run hatch without the macOS client cutting off or clearing pending state earlier than the run budget.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** macOS `chat.send` and shared chat UI pending-run timeout were shorter than the first-run hatch budget, making slow first replies look stuck or prematurely timed out.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw task worktree in this Linux environment, using static source/diff proof because Swift/Xcode tools are not installed here.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `static-chat-timeout-red-proof`, `static-chat-timeout-proof`, and `git diff --check` were run through the validation wrapper.
- **Evidence after fix:**

  ```text
  PASS: baseline mac chat default was 30s
  PASS: baseline reused agent timeout for rpc wait
  PASS: baseline shared pending timeout was 120s
  PASS: baseline did not define five-minute chat default

  PASS: mac agent default 300000
  PASS: mac ack wait remains 30000
  PASS: chatSend default uses agent timeout
  PASS: chatSend payload carries timeout
  PASS: chatSend rpc wait uses ack timeout
  PASS: shared pending timeout 300000
  PASS: mac regression assertion
  PASS: shared regression assertion

  git diff --check passed
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** The macOS chat run default is five minutes, the WebSocket ACK wait remains 30 seconds, the existing `chat.send.timeoutMs` payload carries the run budget, the shared chat UI pending/completion wait is five minutes, and focused Swift regressions assert both client-visible timeout values.
- **What was not tested:** Swift tests were not executed locally because this environment does not include `swift` or `xcodebuild`.
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints:** The local proof is static/diff-based rather than a live macOS app run; CI/macOS runners should execute the added Swift tests.

## Evidence

- Baseline proof confirmed the pre-fix mismatch: macOS chat defaulted the run to 30 seconds, reused that value for the request wait, and shared UI pending timeout was 120 seconds.
- After-fix proof confirmed macOS sends a five-minute run timeout, keeps ACK wait at 30 seconds, and aligns shared UI pending/completion wait to five minutes.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- ClawSweeper commit review passed with nothing found.

## Tests and validation

- **Which commands did you run?** `static-chat-timeout-red-proof`, `static-chat-timeout-proof`, and `git diff --check`.
- **What regression coverage was added or updated?** Added focused Swift regressions for macOS `GatewayConnection.chatSend` default `timeoutMs == 300_000` in the `chat.send` payload and shared `OpenClawChatViewModel` run completion wait using `300_000` ms.
- **Target test file:** `apps/macos/Tests/OpenClawIPCTests/GatewayConnectionControlTests.swift` and `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/ChatViewModelTests.swift`.
- **Scenario locked in:** Default macOS chat send must give the assistant run a five-minute budget while shared UI completion wait uses the same five-minute budget.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The tests assert the exact client-visible payload and UI wait parameter at the two affected client boundaries, so future changes that collapse ACK timeout and run timeout again should fail near the regression source.

## Risk checklist

- **What is the highest-risk area?** Timeout/default behavior for macOS `chat.send` and shared chat UI pending state.
- **How is that risk mitigated?** The ACK/request wait remains short at 30 seconds, so unreachable gateway failures still surface promptly; only the assistant run budget and pending-run UI wait are lengthened to match terminal hatch.
- **Risk labels considered:** public-contract/protocol-adjacent and availability/security-boundary signals were considered because `chat.send.timeoutMs` controls run duration.
- **Risk explanation:** This uses the existing `chat.send.timeoutMs` field and does not add or rename wire fields. The risk is limited to allowing default macOS chat runs to continue longer before timing out.
- **Why acceptable:** Five minutes is already the terminal hatch budget for the same first-run assistant path, and the shorter WebSocket ACK timeout is preserved.

## Current review state

- **What is the next action?** Run CI/macOS Swift tests after PR submission.
- **Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?** New PR; no PR comments yet.
- ClawSweeper commit review passed with nothing found.
- Local validation passed for static red/green timeout proof and `git diff --check`.

target.title: fix(agents): enable bundled static catalog fallback for cron Attempt 2
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Plugin-provided models (e.g. opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash) in the bundled static catalog are now resolvable by non-plugin-harness cron sessions via the embedded runner's Attempt 2 model resolution path.

- **Problem**: Non-plugin-harness isolated cron sessions fail with "Unknown model" for plugin-provided models, even though the models exist in the bundled static catalog. Attempt 2 `resolveModelAsync` is called without `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback`, so the static catalog is never consulted.
- **Solution**: Pass `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback: true` in the Attempt 2 `resolveModelAsync` call, enabling the static catalog fallback path for models that agent discovery cannot resolve.
- **What changed**: `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts` — 4 lines added.
- **What did NOT change**: Attempt 1 behavior (still `skipAgentDiscovery: true`, `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` still gated on `pluginHarnessOwnsTransport`); Attempt 2 structure; model discovery ordering.
- Fixes #95500

## Evidence

### Root cause

The embedded runner's two-attempt model resolution in `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts:1070-1125`:

1. **Attempt 1** (line 1074-1096): `skipAgentDiscovery: true`, `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` only set when `pluginHarnessOwnsTransport === true` (cron sessions: `false`)
2. If Attempt 1 fails, `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` is called (line 1108)
3. **Attempt 2** (line 1111-1123): `resolveModelAsync` called with only `{ workspaceDir, authProfileId }` — **no** `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback`

**Provenance**: `joshavant` added `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` in `da4671ebccfb`. Attempt 1 gates it on `pluginHarnessOwnsTransport`, but Attempt 2 never sets it. The isolated cron executor now routes plugin-provided models through the embedded runner, exposing this gap.

**Confidence**: `clear` — source inspection confirms a single missing boolean flag at a single call site.

### Real behavior proof — full Attempt 1 → 2 chain

The proof exercises the exact `resolveModelAsync` production function called by `runEmbeddedAgentInternal` (run.ts:1074-1125), with `skipAgentDiscovery: true` + `skipProviderRuntimeHooks: true` to simulate the isolated cron environment. The ONLY variable is `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback`.

**Environment**: Linux x86_64, Node v22.11.0, branch `fix/static-catalog-fallback-95500`

```console
$ node --import tsx proof-96070-final.ts
========================================================================
  DEFINITIVE PROOF — PR #96070
  Mirroring runEmbeddedAgentInternal:1074-1125
  Platform: linux Node: v22.11.0
========================================================================

  Provider: opencode-go  Model: deepseek-v4-flash
  Isolation: skipAgentDiscovery + empty runtime hooks
  (simulates cron — plugin hooks absent, only static catalog remains)

── Phase 1: Attempt 1 (run.ts:1091-1096) ──
    skipAgentDiscovery=true, no static catalog fallback
    model: null
    verdict: ❌ null — expected for cron

── Phase 2: ensureOpenClawModelsJson (run.ts:1108) ──
    (regenerates models.json — does not add plugin provider models)

── Phase 3: Attempt 2 WITHOUT allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback ──
    run.ts:1117-1123 on CURRENT MAIN — BUG
    model: null
    verdict: ❌ null — BUG REPRODUCED

── Phase 4: Attempt 2 WITH allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback=true ──
    run.ts:1117 THIS PR's 4-line fix
    model: opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash
    verdict: ✅ opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash — FIX WORKS

========================================================================
  RESULT MATRIX
========================================================================

  Phase | Description                     | allowBundledSC | Model
  ------|---------------------------------|---------------|------
  1     | Attempt 1 (skipDiscovery)       | not set       | null
  2     | ensureOpenClawModelsJson        | —             | regenerated
  3     | Attempt 2 WITHOUT fix (main)    | not set       | null
  4     | Attempt 2 WITH fix (this PR)    | true          | opencode-go/deepseek-v4-flash

  Phase 3 ❌ + Phase 4 ✅ = ✅ PROOF VALID
```

### Source code comparison — main vs this PR

| Call site | Current main | This PR |
|---|---|---|
| Attempt 1 `skipAgentDiscovery` | `true` | unchanged |
| Attempt 1 `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` | `pluginHarnessOwnsTransport` | unchanged |
| Attempt 2 `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback` | (not set — static catalog never consulted) | **`true`** ← the fix |
| `ensureOpenClawModelsJson` between attempts | called | unchanged |

### Why `resolveModelAsync` proof is sufficient

`runEmbeddedAgent` → `runEmbeddedAgentInternal` passes the options object to `resolveModelAsync` **directly** — no transformation, no intermediate logic. The proof calls the same function with the same options shape (the only difference is `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback`, which is precisely the fix). The function is deterministic: same options → same result. An end-to-end cron run would call the same function — same outcome.

### Test results

Existing `model.test.ts` already covers `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback: true` in multiple scenarios (lines 557, 603, 664, 715) — all passing.

### What was NOT tested

Live isolated cron running through the full `runEmbeddedAgent` → gateway chain. The `resolveModelAsync` function is deterministic. The proof covers the exact code path that the cron executor exercises.

## Changes

- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts`: +4 — add `allowBundledStaticCatalogFallback: true` to the Attempt 2 `resolveModelAsync` call

## Regression Test Plan

```console
$ pnpm vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-embedded-agent.config.ts
# model.test.ts passes — includes static catalog fallback coverage
```

target.title: fix(google): add google-gemini-cli auth alias and expand model runtime bindings
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
close #95851
After re-onboarding from another provider (e.g. Claude CLI) to Gemini CLI via `openclaw onboard`, the chat becomes unresponsive — the UI shows infinite loading and never returns a response. The onboarding completed successfully (auth tokens stored, config written), but sending a message hangs indefinitely.

## Why This Change Was Made

- Adds `"providerAuthAliases": { "google-gemini-cli": "google" }` to the Google extension manifest so `resolveProviderIdForAuth("google-gemini-cli")` returns `"google"`. Without this alias, the auth profile fallback path in `resolveCliRuntimeFromAuthProfile()` cannot match `google-gemini-cli` profiles to the `google` provider, returning `undefined` and falling through to a direct Google API call without valid credentials.
- Expands the Gemini CLI config patch from a single model (`google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview`) to all 7 active Gemini chat models. When users pick a model not in the original patch, the `agentRuntime` binding is absent from the `models` map, forcing the broken auth profile fallback path.
- Keeps existing Gemini CLI onboarding for first-time users and direct Google API key auth unchanged.

## User Impact

Users switching from another provider to Gemini CLI via `openclaw onboard` now get working chat responses instead of infinite loading. New Gemini CLI setups and existing Claude CLI / Google API key configs are unaffected.

## Evidence

- Branch base checked during freshness gate: `origin/main` at `ad3b2f4b88`. No final rebase was performed.
- Real behavior proof:
  - **Behavior or issue addressed:** `resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider` returns `"google-gemini-cli"` for Google models after Gemini CLI onboarding, and `resolveProviderIdForAuth("google-gemini-cli")` resolves to `"google"`.
  - **Real environment tested:** Linux Node v22.19.0, OpenClaw `main` branch, branch `fix/gemini-cli-auth-alias-reonboard`.
  - **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
    ```bash
    pnpm test extensions/google/manifest.test.ts extensions/google/index.test.ts src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.test.ts src/agents/provider-auth-aliases.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts --run
    ```
  - **Evidence after fix:**
    ```text
    Test Files  5 passed (5)
         Tests  80 passed (80)

    New test cases:
    - model-runtime-aliases.test.ts: resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider returns "google-gemini-cli"
      when model config binds it to a Google model
    - provider-auth-aliases.test.ts: resolveProviderIdForAuth("google-gemini-cli") returns "google"
      via the manifest providerAuthAliases
    - manifest.test.ts: Google manifest declares providerAuthAliases: { "google-gemini-cli": "google" }
    ```
  - **Observed result after fix:** `resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider({ provider: "google", modelId: "gemini-3.1-pro-preview", cfg: { agents: { defaults: { models: { "google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview": { agentRuntime: { id: "google-gemini-cli" } } } } } } })` returns `"google-gemini-cli"` — the correct CLI execution provider. `resolveProviderIdForAuth("google-gemini-cli", { metadataSnapshot })` returns `"google"`.
  - **What was not tested:** Live Gemini CLI OAuth onboarding with a real Google account sending a chat message end-to-end. The reproduction exercises the production `resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider` and `resolveProviderIdForAuth` functions with real config shapes, but the full OAuth flow and Gemini CLI process execution are not exercised.
- Regression tests and local checks:
  ```text
  pnpm test extensions/google/manifest.test.ts extensions/google/index.test.ts src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.test.ts src/agents/provider-auth-aliases.test.ts src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts --run
  Test Files  5 passed (5)
  Tests  80 passed (80)

  git diff --check origin/main..HEAD
  passed with no output
  ```

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: Unit tests
- Target test files: `src/agents/model-runtime-aliases.test.ts`, `src/agents/provider-auth-aliases.test.ts`, `extensions/google/manifest.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - `resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider` resolves `"google-gemini-cli"` when model config binds it for a Google model
  - `resolveProviderIdForAuth("google-gemini-cli")` returns `"google"` via the manifest `providerAuthAliases`
  - Google manifest declares `providerAuthAliases: { "google-gemini-cli": "google" }`
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bugs are in (1) the manifest metadata declaration and (2) the config patch shape generated during onboarding. The new tests directly verify both points at the level where the bug lived.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Two interacting gaps. (1) The Google extension manifest had no `providerAuthAliases` entry mapping `google-gemini-cli` → `google`, so `resolveCliRuntimeFromAuthProfile()` could not recognize `google-gemini-cli` auth profiles as compatible with the `google` provider — the fallback returned `undefined` and the runtime attempted a direct Google API call without valid credentials. (2) The Gemini CLI config patch only set `agentRuntime` for `google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview`, so any other Gemini model selected during model picking had no runtime binding and fell through to the broken auth profile fallback.
- Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered Anthropic/Claude CLI runtime resolution but not the Gemini CLI `resolveCliRuntimeExecutionProvider → resolveCliRuntimeFromAuthProfile → resolveProviderIdForAuth` path, and no test verified the config patch covered all active models.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: docs: add Codex long-task reliability runbook
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a public Codex long-task reliability runbook based on a field deployment where long Telegram/cron tasks kept appearing stalled or failing even after queue/concurrency tuning.

The report is intentionally sanitized and generic. It documents the key distinction we kept missing as operators:

- `messages.queue.mode=collect` helps direct-chat follow-ups avoid interrupting active turns.
- `cron.maxConcurrentRuns` and `agents.defaults.maxConcurrent` control worker capacity.
- `appServer.requestTimeoutMs` controls Codex app-server control-plane requests.
- `appServer.turnCompletionIdleTimeoutMs` controls the quiet window after turn-scoped app-server requests while OpenClaw waits for `turn/completed`.

The last one is what made long jobs still fail after the other knobs were tuned.

## Field Evidence

Observed on a local macOS LaunchAgent deployment running stable `2026.5.7`:

- `messages.queue.mode: collect`
- `agents.defaults.maxConcurrent: 4`
- `cron.maxConcurrentRuns: 3`
- `plugins.entries.codex.config.appServer.requestTimeoutMs: 1200000`
- repeated logs like:
  - `codex app-server turn idle timed out waiting for completion`
  - `embedded run failover decision ... reason=timeout ... rawError=codex app-server attempt timed out`
  - occasional `timeout-compaction` when direct-session context was high

Operator mitigations tried before this doc:

- raised `appServer.requestTimeoutMs`
- kept Telegram queueing in `collect`
- increased local concurrency
- moved explicit long work to isolated cron jobs
- added checkpoint files and progress conventions
- reset high-context direct Telegram sessions

Those helped queueing and recovery but did not fully address the Codex turn-completion quiet-window failure mode.

## What This PR Changes

- Adds `docs/plugins/codex-harness-long-tasks.md` with:
  - quick fix using `turnCompletionIdleTimeoutMs`
  - diagnosis commands
  - symptom table
  - queueing vs durability explanation
  - checkpointing pattern for long background work
  - maintainer follow-up ideas
- Links the new page from Codex harness troubleshooting and the Codex harness reference timeout section.
- Adds the page to Mintlify navigation.

## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `PATH="/usr/local/lib/node_modules/corepack/shims:$PATH" pnpm check:docs`

target.title: fix: add @zed-industries/codex-acp to minimumReleaseAgeExclude
target.body:
## Problem

`@zed-industries/codex-acp` and its platform-specific packages (`@zed-industries/codex-acp-darwin-arm64`, `-x64`, `-linux-arm64`, etc.) are transitive dependencies that release frequently. The `minimumReleaseAge: 2880` (48-hour) guard in `pnpm-workspace.yaml` blocks `pnpm install` whenever a new version ships less than 48 hours before a scheduled install run, causing the install to fail.

## Fix

Add `@zed-industries/codex-acp` and `@zed-industries/codex-acp-*` to `minimumReleaseAgeExclude`, matching the pattern already used for `@openai/codex` and `@openai/codex-*`.

## Why this pattern is safe

These are native binary packages (Zed ACP runtime). They follow the same release cadence pattern as `@openai/codex-*` which is already excluded. Blocking on release age provides no meaningful safety benefit for first-party tooling packages from known publishers.

target.title: feat(telegram): presence indicator (typing + phased reactions)
target.body:
## Summary
- Adds three-phase presence indicator for Telegram messages: 👀 on receive, 👨‍💻 + typing while processing, cleared on done.
- Eliminates silent-agent UX where users can't tell if a message was received or is still being worked on.
- Per-account config under `channels.telegram[.accounts[*]].presence` (`typing`, `reaction`, `workingReaction`); defaults on for new accounts, backward-compatible.

## Implementation
- New `extensions/telegram/src/presence-indicator.ts` exporting `PresenceIndicator` / `PresenceCtx` and a Telegram-specific factory built on the existing Bot API client and the 401-backoff-safe ``sendChatAction`` handler.
- Hook points:
  - ``onMessageReceived`` fires inside ``bot.on('message')`` and ``bot.on('channel_post')`` in ``bot-handlers.runtime.ts``, before queueing/dispatch.
  - ``onProcessingStart`` / ``onProcessingEnd`` wrap ``dispatchTelegramMessage`` in ``bot-message.ts`` using try/finally so the reaction is always cleared.
- Edge cases handled per spec: 400ms debounce on typing, ref-counted per (chatId, threadId) so concurrent processors share one loop, warn-and-swallow on Telegram API failures, and a 60s TTL watchdog that clears orphan reactions if ``onProcessingEnd`` never fires.

## Test plan
- [x] Unit (``presence-indicator.test.ts``): lifecycle order, typing-loop interval, <400ms debounce, reaction state, failure tolerance, ref-counted concurrency, orphan watchdog.
- [x] Config: ``presence.typing: false`` disables the loop; ``presence.reaction: null`` disables reactions entirely; custom emoji overrides respected.
- [x] Processor (``bot-message.test.ts``): ``onProcessingStart`` runs before dispatch and ``onProcessingEnd`` after, including the dispatch-throws path.
- [ ] Manual: send a message via a test bot, verify 👀 → 👨‍💻 → cleared with "typing..." visible.

## Notes
- ``setMessageReaction`` was already wired in the channel; this PR adds an additional explicit lifecycle on top, gated by config so existing ``ackReaction`` users are unaffected when ``presence`` is absent.
- Pre-existing unrelated test failures in ``setup-surface.test.ts``, ``bot.create-telegram-bot.test.ts (pairing)``, ``channel.message-adapter.test.ts``, and ``extensions/whatsapp/src/login.coverage.test.ts`` reproduce on clean ``main`` in this environment (profile-name string mismatches, vitest mock-type drift). Not introduced here.

Reported by @kotaro.

target.title: fix(memory-lancedb): add retry mechanism for Windows Docker bind mount sync delays
target.body:
Hey! 👋

This PR hardens the memory-lancedb plugin initialization path for transient LanceDB table metadata errors seen with Docker bind-mounted data directories.

## The Problem

When a LanceDB data directory is backed by a host bind mount, table metadata can be briefly unavailable after table creation. The previous one-shot initialization path could fail during startup, and the first retry version still had two gaps:

- a table created before `__schema__` cleanup could be reopened and used before cleanup was retried
- the bind-mount guidance was gated on `process.platform === "win32"`, which misses Linux containers running on Docker Desktop

## The Solution

The retry loop now keeps the table local until cleanup succeeds, retries cleanup when a table is reopened, and surfaces generic Docker bind-mount guidance after retry exhaustion.

## What Changed

- Updated `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.ts` to only cache the table after `__schema__` cleanup succeeds.
- Added Docker bind-mount guidance on retry exhaustion regardless of host/runtime platform.
- Added focused coverage in `extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts` for transient create-cleanup failure and exhausted retry diagnostics.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** memory-lancedb can initialize a LanceDB-backed memory table, store a memory, and recall it after this initialization retry hardening.

**Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw plugin registration path using the actual memory-lancedb plugin, actual `@lancedb/lancedb` storage in a temporary database, and a local OpenAI-compatible embeddings endpoint.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```console
$ NODE_OPTIONS=--no-deprecation node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-memory-lancedb-real-smoke.ts
```

**Evidence after fix:** copied live console output from the real smoke run:

```console
OpenClaw memory-lancedb real setup smoke
dbPath: <temp>/lancedb
registered tools: memory_forget, memory_recall, memory_store
embedding requests: 2
store action: created
stored id format: uuid
recall count: 1
recalled category: preference
recalled text: I prefer deterministic OpenClaw memory proof runs.
startup log: info:memory-lancedb: plugin registered (db: <temp>/lancedb, lazy init)
```

**Observed result after fix:** `memory_store` created a UUID-backed memory in the LanceDB table, and `memory_recall` returned one recalled preference from that same temporary LanceDB database.

**What was not tested:** I did not run a Windows Docker Desktop bind-mount reproduction locally. The real smoke covers the actual plugin + LanceDB store/recall path; the focused regression tests cover transient cleanup retry and the exhausted-retry Docker bind-mount diagnostic.

## Testing

```console
$ pnpm test extensions/memory-lancedb/index.test.ts
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  46 passed (46)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 12.34s
```

```console

All matched files use the correct format.
```

Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
```

# no output
```

target.title: Filter assistant process chatter from Dreaming session corpus
target.body:
Fixes #80664. Adds a conservative Dreaming session-corpus hygiene gate that rejects obvious assistant internal process chatter (e.g. `Assistant: Need commit PR`, `Assistant: Now inspect`, `Assistant: Oops ... poll`) before snippets enter session-corpus recall and Light/REM/Deep candidate surfaces, while preserving explicit durable signals such as Arthur/user decisions, verified/PASS artifacts, PR numbers, and commit SHAs.

Validation: added focused tests in `dreaming-phases.test.ts`. Local targeted vitest could not run in the original clone because Corepack/pnpm reported `spawnSync pnpm ENOENT` during dependency setup, so tests are included for CI. After that, this branch was also exercised with the real Dreaming light-session ingestion path against a temporary redacted OpenClaw workspace; proof below.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Dreaming session-corpus ingestion should reject obvious assistant internal process chatter before it can enter session-corpus recall / Light / REM / Deep candidate surfaces, while preserving durable decision and verification signals.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw branch `memory-dreaming-session-corpus-filter` at commit `55b333c6b604df07016a050cb67d3342f8d961ef`, run on a Linux OpenClaw workspace clone with Node `v22.22.0`. The run created a temporary real OpenClaw workspace under `/tmp`, wrote a redacted session transcript under that workspace's `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, then invoked the branch's Dreaming light-session ingestion hook.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
cd /root/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/pr80666-proof/openclaw
git checkout pr-80666
git rev-parse HEAD  # 55b333c6b604df07016a050cb67d3342f8d961ef
node --import tsx tmp/pr80666-real-proof.ts | tee /root/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/pr80666-proof/proof-output.txt
```

- Evidence after fix (terminal capture / copied live output, paths redacted):

```text
PR #80666 Dreaming session-corpus real-workspace proof
repo_head=/root/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/pr80666-proof/openclaw
workspace=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED
state_dir=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED/.state
session_transcript=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED/.state/agents/main/sessions/real-pr80666-redacted-session.jsonl
hook_result={"handled":true,"reason":"memory-core: light dreaming processed"}
corpus_path=/tmp/openclaw-pr80666-real-workspace-REDACTED/memory/.dreams/session-corpus/2026-05-12.txt
rejected:PASS_ABSENT:Assistant: Need commit PR.
rejected:PASS_ABSENT:Assistant: Now inspect.
rejected:PASS_ABSENT:Assistant: Oops worktree maybe not created yet due first command still running. poll.
preserved:PASS_PRESENT:Arthur confirmed PR #80666 accepted; verification PASS; commit c33055e8e2ddc12a3ccdac28cfcf0ad5e1be5940.
preserved:PASS_PRESENT:Assistant: The durable decision is recorded with PR #80666 and PASS evidence.
--- redacted corpus excerpt ---
[main/sessions/main/real-pr80666-redacted-session.jsonl#L4] User: Arthur confirmed PR #80666 accepted; verification PASS; commit c33055e8e2ddc12a3ccdac28cfcf0ad5e1be5940.
[main/sessions/main/real-pr80666-redacted-session.jsonl#L5] Assistant: Assistant: The durable decision is recorded with PR #80666 and PASS evidence.
--- end excerpt ---
```

- Observed result after fix: The generated `memory/.dreams/session-corpus/2026-05-12.txt` omitted all three assistant process-chatter samples (`Need commit PR`, `Now inspect`, and `Oops ... poll`) and retained the durable `Arthur confirmed PR #80666 ... PASS ... commit c33055e8...` signal plus an assistant-authored durable PR/PASS line. This shows the new ingestion-boundary filter runs before the session-corpus artifact is written, while durable PR / PASS / commit evidence is still available to later Dreaming candidate surfaces.
- What was not tested: Full production user data and screenshot/recording capture were not used; the live output above used a temporary redacted workspace and copied terminal output. Full CI remains supplemental.

target.title: fix(swift): keep device auth compatible with v2 gateways
target.body:
## Summary
- Add a Swift OpenClawKit v2 device-auth payload builder alongside the existing v3 builder.
- Sign gateway connect requests with the v2 canonical payload for compatibility with deployed gateways that have not yet upgraded to v3 verification.
- Add reset helpers for device identity/auth store and a regression test for the v2 payload vector.

## Why
Current gateway server code can verify both v3 and v2, but existing deployed gateways may still verify v2 only. A Swift client that signs v3-only can be rejected before a pending pairing request is created, surfacing as Approval Pending or device signature invalid in downstream apps.

Using the v2 payload keeps current Swift clients compatible with older deployed gateways while newer gateways continue to accept the signature.

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Swift OpenClawKit clients could sign gateway connect with the v3 device-auth payload while an already-deployed gateway verified only the v2 canonical payload. The live result was a websocket close before pairing, leaving the downstream app stuck in Approval Pending because no pending device existed to approve.

**Real environment tested:** Downstream Rem iOS simulator connected to a live per-user Fly gateway running an older deployed OpenClaw gateway image. Private gateway hostname, user ID, device ID, token, public key, and signature are redacted.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Built and ran the downstream iOS app with this patched OpenClawKit commit, connected it to the live Fly gateway, opened Settings > Gateways, triggered explicit managed-cloud re-pair, watched app runtime logs, backend approval logs, and gateway websocket behavior, then visually confirmed the gateway status in the app.

**Evidence after fix:** Redacted runtime log excerpt from the patched downstream app/live Fly gateway validation:

```text
before patch:
gateway websocket closed before connect: code=1008 reason=device signature invalid
gateway paired devices: existing cli/operator record only
gateway pending devices: []
backend approve-device: auto-approve timed out: no pending devices found

after patch:
gateway connect returned: pairing required
backend approve-device: requesting auto-approve from backend
backend approve-device: auto-approve succeeded, reconnecting
gateway session: operator session connected
app Settings > Gateways: Connected
```

**Observed result after fix:** The live gateway moved from device-signature-invalid before pairing to pairing required, backend auto-approve succeeded, the operator session connected, and the downstream app showed Settings > Gateways as Connected.

**What was not tested:** I did not upgrade every managed Fly gateway image or test every self-managed gateway variant. The proof covers one real older deployed Fly gateway that reproduced the v2-only verification failure, plus the Swift payload-vector test for the canonical v2 string.

## Validation
- SwiftPM DeviceAuthPayloadTests passed.
- Downstream iOS build and simulator run passed.
- Downstream live Fly gateway validation passed after switching Swift connect signing to the v2 canonical payload.

target.title: [codex] fix WhatsApp reconnect catch-up replies
target.body:
## Summary

- Keep the existing short stale-history guard for initial WhatsApp Web startup history sync.
- After reconnecting from a recently active WhatsApp listener, allow recent `messages.upsert(type="append")` catch-up messages to enter the auto-reply path within the existing message watchdog window.
- Add regression coverage for the inbox append grace window and the reconnect handoff from the Web monitor.
- Document the reconnect catch-up behavior in the WhatsApp runtime model and add a changelog entry.

## Root Cause

OpenClaw already received Baileys `append` upserts for history/offline catch-up, but `extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/monitor.ts` only auto-replied when the message timestamp was within 60 seconds before the new socket's connect time. During a stale socket/reconnect window, a real inbound message can be several minutes older than the replacement socket's connect time. In that case OpenClaw marked the message read and skipped auto-reply as stale startup history.

Refs #50093.

## Verification

- `pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.behavior.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/channels/whatsapp.md extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.test-harness.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply.web-auto-reply.connection-and-logging.e2e.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/inbound/monitor.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/monitor-inbox.append-upsert.test-support.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm changed:lanes --json`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Notes

I did not do a live WhatsApp-device replay because the original miss was observed on a personal local setup and GitHub auth required pushing through a fork. The focused tests cover the exact skipped `append` window and the reconnect option wiring.

target.title: test(config): add unit tests for mapZodIssueToConfigIssue
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The `validation.ts` module provides config validation for OpenClaw runtime settings, including the `mapZodIssueToConfigIssue` function that transforms raw Zod validation issues into user-facing `ConfigValidationIssue` objects. Despite being a core path in config validation, this function had no direct unit tests, with only 2 indirect tests through higher-level validation flows.

## Why This Change Was Made

Add focused unit tests for `mapZodIssueToConfigIssue` to verify:
- Basic path and message mapping (string/numeric paths, missing messages)
- Numeric bound hints (too_big/too_small with inclusive/exclusive boundaries)
- Allowed values collection (invalid_value, invalid_type→boolean, custom `expected one of` patterns, invalid_union branches)
- Allowed values hidden count when values exceed 12
- Bindings-specific union extraction with route-type-mismatch filtering
- Edge cases (null/undefined input, non-array paths, malformed errors)

This improves test coverage and prevents regressions when the function is modified.

## User Impact

Config validation issue formatting now has 34 dedicated unit tests covering the main branching paths, reducing the risk of regressions.

## Evidence

target.title: fix(telegram): make pre-connect network failures recoverable in send context
target.body:
## Summary

grammy's Telegram bot library can emit bare `"Network request for 'X' failed!"` errors for pre-connect failures (DNS resolution, TLS handshake, etc.) — errors that are safe to retry since the request never reached Telegram's servers.

The existing regex `GRAMMY_NETWORK_REQUEST_FAILED_AFTER_RE` required the `"\s+after\b"` clause, so these bare pre-connect failures were not classified as recoverable in `context: send` and messages were dropped instead of retried.

## Fix

Relax the regex to make `"after"` optional:

```diff
- const GRAMMY_NETWORK_REQUEST_FAILED_AFTER_RE =
-   /^network request(?:\s+for\s+["']?[^"']+["']?)?\s+failed\s+after .*[!.]?$/i;
+ const GRAMMY_NETWORK_REQUEST_FAILED_AFTER_RE =
+   /^network request(?:\s+for\s+["']?[^"']+["']?)?\s+failed(?:\s+after .*)?[!.]?$/i;
```

| Message | Before | After |
|---------|--------|-------|
| `Network request for 'sendMessage' failed after 2 attempts.` | ✅ recoverable | ✅ recoverable |
| `Network request for 'sendMessage' failed!` | ❌ dropped | ✅ recoverable |
| `Network request for 'X' failed!` | ❌ dropped | ✅ recoverable |

The existing test case covering `"failed after N attempts."` continues to pass — only the pre-connect failure path is corrected.

## Verification

- Regex behavior verified against known grammy error message patterns
- Existing `"failed after N attempts."` test case still passes (same behavior)
- TypeScript syntax check: clean
- 2 files changed, 5 insertions, 3 deletions

Closes #80362

target.title: Harden ACP worker dispatch controls
target.body:
## Summary

- inject Claude and Gemini ACP model choices at process launch instead of replaying unsupported runtime config after session start
- skip unsupported Claude/Gemini runtime config replays for model, thinking/effort, and timeout controls
- allow `sessions_yield` to succeed when the gateway has an agent session context but no in-turn `onYield` callback

## Context

This fixes a Foreman dispatch failure mode where a spawned ACP worker was accepted, then the child agent failed shortly after start with `ACP_TURN_FAILED` while the parent believed the worker was still running. For Claude ACP, the launcher can now bake the requested model into the subprocess environment and avoid the post-init `setModel` path. For Gemini ACP, the requested model is passed with `--model` at launch.

## Tests

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extension-acpx.config.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts`
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs src/agents/tools/sessions-yield-tool.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/acpx/src/runtime.ts extensions/acpx/src/runtime.test.ts src/agents/openclaw-tools.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-yield-tool.ts src/agents/tools/sessions-yield-tool.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:core`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
