target.title: fix(env): propagate service-managed GH_TOKEN to trusted exec
target.body: ## Summary - Problem: `~/.openclaw/.env` could not be used as a durable source for operator-curated tool secrets like `GH_TOKEN` because override-only-dangerous keys were stripped before the managed gateway service could expose them to trusted exec children. - Why it matters: operators can configure `gh` and similar CLI tools for OpenClaw, but after gateway install/restart those tools could not see `GH_TOKEN` through the managed service path, so authenticated CLI workflows failed unless the token was injected some other way. - What changed: allow `~/.openclaw/.env` to retain override-only-blocked secrets as managed service keys, add a trusted exec inherited-env allowlist sourced from `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS`, and document the durable service env behavior. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): everywhere-dangerous env vars such as `LD_PRELOAD`, `NODE_OPTIONS`, `BASH_ENV`, `DYLD_*`, and `GIT_DIR` stay blocked. `OPENCLAW_*` keys still do not gain trusted exec inheritance. Untrusted exec postures do not receive these secrets. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [x] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [x] Skills / tool execution - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: `GH_TOKEN` placed in `~/.openclaw/.env` should survive managed gateway install/restart and be available to trusted gateway exec children, so `gh` can authenticate without manually injecting the token into each command. - Real environment tested: macOS OpenClaw gateway installed as a managed service, local OpenClaw checkout from v2026.5.2 with this patch applied, `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`, Telegram/agent exec path using `host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: ```sh cp ~/.env ~/.openclaw/.env chmod 600 ~/.openclaw/.env openclaw gateway stop openclaw gateway install --force openclaw gateway start openclaw gateway status ``` then ask the agent to run `gh auth status` and report back - Evidence after fix (screenshot): - Telegram screenshot showing the live exec environment reporting `GH_TOKEN_present: true`, `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`, `gh auth status` logged in via `GH_TOKEN`, and `gh api user` returning the authenticated account JSON. Account details and token values are redacted. - Observed result after fix: trusted gateway exec can use `GH_TOKEN` inherited from the managed service env, and `gh` authenticates successfully from the OpenClaw exec environment. - What was not tested: Linux/systemd real-service behavior was not manually tested; the Linux path is covered by the shared service-env/unit tests. Non-trusted exec postures were verified by targeted unit tests, not by a second live service screenshot. The exec integration test (`bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts`) is skipped on Windows (shell-dependent); unit-level coverage runs on all platforms. - Before evidence (optional but encouraged): - `before-1.jpg` and `before-2.jpg` — terminal screenshots showing the pre-fix managed-service behavior where the gateway env path stripped `GH_TOKEN` before it could reach the exec child, so `gh` could not authenticate after service install/restart. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: `~/.openclaw/.env` parsing treated keys that are dangerous as agent request overrides the same as keys that are dangerous everywhere. This stripped operator-curated tool tokens like `GH_TOKEN` before the managed service could record them as durable env. Separately, exec env sanitization had no trusted-mode inherited-key allowlist, so there was no narrow path for managed service keys to reach trusted tool children. - Missing detection / guardrail: existing tests covered dangerous env stripping and service env formatting, but did not cover the desired operator-curated secret path from `~/.openclaw/.env` through managed service metadata into trusted gateway exec. - Contributing context (if known): the security model already distinguished inherited/env override risk, but the durable service env path did not preserve that distinction. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [x] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: - `src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts` - `src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts` - `src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts` - `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts` - `src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `GH_TOKEN` and similar override-only-blocked keys can be retained from owner-controlled durable service env, become managed service keys, and reach exec children only in the trusted gateway posture (`host=gateway`, `security=full`, `ask=off`). Unsafe keys and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: unit tests cover each policy seam, and the exec integration test proves the env-composition wiring reaches the child process under the relevant exec postures without requiring live GitHub credentials in CI. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A. - If no new test is added, why not: N/A, new tests were added. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Operators can place `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env`, reinstall/start the managed gateway, and use `gh` from trusted gateway exec. This does not grant those tokens to untrusted exec postures. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser strips override-only-dangerous key -> managed gateway service has no GH_TOKEN-managed allowlist entry -> trusted exec child cannot authenticate gh After: ~/.openclaw/.env GH_TOKEN -> durable env parser keeps operator-curated override-only key -> install records GH_TOKEN in OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS -> trusted gateway exec allowlist permits inherited GH_TOKEN -> gh authenticates from the exec child ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No. - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No. - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this intentionally allows operator-curated service env secrets like `GH_TOKEN` to reach trusted gateway exec children. The path is constrained to keys recorded by the managed service env metadata and to the trusted exec posture. Everywhere-dangerous keys are still stripped, `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded, request overrides remain sanitized, and non-trusted postures do not inherit the allowlisted secret. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local managed OpenClaw gateway service - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw agent exec path, GitHub CLI (`gh`) as the real CLI consumer - Relevant config (redacted): `GH_TOKEN` present in `~/.openclaw/.env`; `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` included `GH_TOKEN`; token value redacted in all evidence. ### Steps 1. Put `GH_TOKEN` in `~/.openclaw/.env` and set owner-only permissions. 2. Reinstall/restart the managed gateway service with this patch applied. 3. Run a trusted gateway exec env probe and `gh auth status` / `gh api user`. 4. Run the targeted regression tests: ```sh pnpm test src/daemon/service-managed-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-trusted-env.exec.test.ts src/config/state-dir-dotenv.test.ts src/infra/host-env-security.test.ts src/commands/daemon-install-helpers.test.ts ``` ### Expected - `process.env.GH_TOKEN` is present inside trusted gateway exec. - `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` contains `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` reports authentication via `GH_TOKEN`. - Untrusted exec postures do not receive the fixture token in tests. - Everywhere-dangerous and `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. ### Actual - Live evidence showed `GH_TOKEN_present: true` and `service_managed_keys_contains_GH_TOKEN: true`. - `gh auth status` authenticated via `GH_TOKEN` and `gh api user` succeeded with redacted account output. - Targeted tests covered the parser, managed service allowlist, host env sanitizer, daemon install plan, and exec wiring. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Evidence files prepared for upload: - `before-1.jpg` - `before-2.jpg` - `after-1.jpg` ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: - Managed gateway install/restart picks up `GH_TOKEN` from `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Trusted gateway exec sees `GH_TOKEN` and `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` includes `GH_TOKEN`. - `gh auth status` and `gh api user` work from the OpenClaw exec environment using `GH_TOKEN`. - Targeted tests exercise trusted, allowlist, and prompted exec postures plus dangerous-key exclusions. - Edge cases checked: - `OPENCLAW_*` keys are excluded from trusted inherited allowlist. - Everywhere-dangerous keys remain blocked even if listed. - PATH override smuggling remains blocked. - Default sanitizer behavior remains strict without `allowInheritedKeys`. - What you did **not** verify: - Manual Linux/systemd live service verification. - Every provider token named in the override-only dangerous list; `GH_TOKEN` is the live proof case and tests use representative fixture keys. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes. - Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) Yes, for operators who want this behavior. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: place `GH_TOKEN` or similar operator-curated tool secret in `~/.openclaw/.env`, ensure the file is owner-only, then reinstall/restart the managed gateway service so `OPENCLAW_SERVICE_MANAGED_ENV_KEYS` is regenerated. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: secrets that were previously stripped from managed service env can now reach trusted exec children. - Mitigation: only operator-curated durable service env keys are considered, only trusted gateway exec posture receives the inherited allowlist, and dangerous-everywhere plus `OPENCLAW_*` keys remain blocked. - Risk: operators may assume all exec postures receive `GH_TOKEN` after placing it in `~/.openclaw/.env`. - Mitigation: docs state that the exec layer decides inheritance by posture and that these keys are not auto-propagated to untrusted commands.

target.title: feat(mattermost): add automatic ack reactions
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Mattermost supported typing, preview streaming, and manual `message action=react`, but it did not send automatic ack reactions for accepted inbound messages.
- Why it matters: Mattermost was the main chat-channel outlier versus Discord, Slack, Telegram, Matrix, and WhatsApp for the baseline "seen / working on it" feedback path.
- What changed: added Mattermost automatic ack reactions, Mattermost emoji-name normalization for common unicode defaults, optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction` / `channels.mattermost.accounts.<id>.ackReaction` overrides, and the matching generated config-surface artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no Mattermost lifecycle status reactions, no changes to typing behavior, and no changes to draft preview streaming.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: automatic Mattermost ack reactions for accepted inbound messages, including config override resolution and optional ack cleanup after reply.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw maintainer worktree on Linux with Mattermost-focused tests, build, generated-config checks, the full changed-scope gate, and a live Mattermost workspace smoke on this exact branch.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `pnpm test extensions/mattermost/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reactions.test.ts extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/ack-reactions.test.ts`
  - `pnpm build`
  - `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata`
  - `pnpm config:docs:check`
  - `pnpm check:changed`
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  20 passed (20)

pnpm build -> passed
pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata -> passed
pnpm config:docs:check -> OK docs/.generated/config-baseline.sha256
pnpm check:changed -> passed

Live Mattermost smoke on this branch -> passed
- started the branch with `pnpm gateway:watch`
- configured `messages.ackReactionScope=all` and `channels.mattermost.ackReaction=eyes`
- sent real Mattermost messages in the connected workspace
- observed visible ack reactions in Mattermost
- no ack/reaction failure strings appeared in the runtime logs
```
- Observed result after fix: Mattermost now has an automatic ack reaction path wired into inbound monitoring, common unicode ack emoji are normalized to Mattermost emoji names, config/docs/generated surfaces are aligned, and visible ack reactions were confirmed in a live Mattermost workspace on this exact branch.
- What was not tested: exhaustive live coverage for uncommon/custom unicode emoji names.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, Mattermost only supported manual `message action=react`; there was no automatic inbound ack reaction path in the monitor.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: N/A. This is a scoped feature/parity improvement, not a regression fix.
- Missing detection / guardrail: the first implementation pass missed the generated bundled channel metadata and config baseline hash that need to move with a public config-surface change.
- Contributing context (if known): Mattermost has a preview-streaming-first design, so this change intentionally stops at the ack layer instead of adding full status-reaction lifecycle UI.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/ack-reactions.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: account/channel/global ack resolution, mention-scope gating, emoji normalization, and `removeAckAfterReply` cleanup behavior.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the existing Mattermost websocket monitor harness hangs in this worktree, so the smallest stable coverage is the new ack helper unit coverage plus generated-surface/build/changed-gate proof.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `extensions/mattermost/src/mattermost/reactions.test.ts` now covers emoji normalization through the reaction transport.
- If no new test is added, why not: a monitor-level websocket assertion was attempted and dropped because the existing harness hangs; I kept stable unit coverage rather than land a flaky test.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Mattermost can now add automatic ack reactions for accepted inbound messages.
- `messages.removeAckAfterReply` now applies to Mattermost ack reactions too.
- Mattermost now supports optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction` and `channels.mattermost.accounts.<id>.ackReaction` overrides.
- Common unicode ack emoji like `👀` are normalized to Mattermost emoji names automatically.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[inbound Mattermost message] -> [mention/access gating] -> [typing + preview streaming] -> [reply]

After:
[inbound Mattermost message] -> [mention/access gating] -> [auto ack reaction] -> [typing + preview streaming] -> [reply] -> [optional ack removal]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`No`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:
  - The new network calls are the existing Mattermost reaction add/remove API calls, sent only for accepted inbound messages when ack reactions are enabled. They are gated by normal Mattermost access checks, `messages.ackReaction*`, and `channels.mattermost.actions.reactions`.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux
- Runtime/container: local Node 24 worktree
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted): `messages.ackReaction`, `messages.ackReactionScope`, `messages.removeAckAfterReply`, optional `channels.mattermost.ackReaction`

### Steps

1. Configure `messages.ackReaction` and `messages.ackReactionScope` for Mattermost.
2. Send an accepted inbound Mattermost message that passes normal access/mention gating.
3. Observe ack reaction creation, then send a reply path that optionally removes the ack when `removeAckAfterReply` is enabled.

### Expected

- Mattermost adds the configured ack reaction when the inbound message qualifies.
- Common unicode defaults normalize to Mattermost emoji names.
- Ack cleanup only happens after a visible reply is delivered.

### Actual

- Verified via new unit coverage, generated-surface checks, build, `pnpm check:changed`, and a live Mattermost smoke on this exact branch that showed visible ack reactions plus live ack removal after visible replies.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: config override resolution, mention-scope gating, common emoji normalization, `removeAckAfterReply`, `actions.reactions=false`, generated config metadata, config baseline hash, build, the full changed-scope gate, live Mattermost ack creation on this exact branch, and live ack removal after visible replies.
- Edge cases checked: no ack when the message is dropped before dispatch, no ack removal when no reply is delivered, and no default-group ack for open `chatmode: "onmessage"` rooms when scope remains `group-mentions`.
- What you did **not** verify: uncommon/custom emoji names beyond the common normalized set.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes`)
- Config/env changes? (`Yes`)
- Migration needed? (`No`)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk:
  - Mattermost shortcode normalization is not exhaustive for every possible unicode emoji.
  - Mitigation:
    - Common/default ack emoji are normalized explicitly, and raw shortcode config still works.
- Risk:
  - Live proof does not exhaustively cover uncommon/custom emoji names.
  - Mitigation:
    - Added focused unit coverage, ran build, refreshed generated config artifacts, passed `pnpm check:changed`, confirmed live ack creation and live cleanup after visible replies, and verified that `NO_REPLY` leaves the ack in place as expected.

target.title: feat(android): add chat agent selector
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the Android chat surface could show multiple agents indirectly, but users had no explicit way to choose which configured gateway agent should receive new chat messages.
- Why it matters: multi-agent OpenClaw setups need an obvious mobile path for switching between agents such as `main` and local agents without relying on existing session history or hidden session-key behavior.
- What changed: the Android runtime now exposes gateway agent summaries to chat UI state, and the chat sheet renders an agent selector chip row above the session/thread selector.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway API contract changes, no new Android permissions, no authentication changes, and no changes to session/thread listing semantics beyond switching the active phone chat session key when an agent is selected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Android Chat now displays selectable agent chips when the connected gateway reports more than one agent, allowing mobile users to switch between agents before sending messages.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw gateway with multiple configured agents (`main` plus a local Ollama-backed agent), Android debug build generated from this branch and sideloaded/tested on a physical Android phone.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Build Android debug APK from this branch.
  2. Install/sideload the debug APK on the Android phone.
  3. Connect the app to the local OpenClaw gateway.
  4. Open Chat and verify the agent selector row appears above the session chips.
  5. Select the alternate agent and send a chat message.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):
  - Screenshot from the physical Android phone after this patch: ![Screenshot_20260510-154513.png](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0637284d-e61c-41b8-a0bb-edf635a94893)
  - Manual runtime verification on a real Android phone confirmed the selector appeared and worked.
  - Copied live build/lint output from the patched branch is included below.
- Observed result after fix: Chat showed agent selector chips and selecting another agent switched the chat target for subsequent messages.
- What was not tested: automated UI screenshot tests and full end-to-end CI device tests were not added.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): before this patch, Android Chat exposed session chips only; there was no first-class agent selector in the Chat sheet.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature addition, not a bug fix or regression.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): multi-agent gateway data was already fetched for the home canvas, but it was not exposed to the Chat UI.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A — this is a feature addition, not a bug fix or regression.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
 - [ ] Unit test
 - [ ] Seam / integration test
 - [ ] End-to-end test
 - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: N/A
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: this is a small UI wiring change using existing gateway `agents.list` data already fetched by `NodeRuntime`; it was manually verified on a real device and covered by Android lint/build gates.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Android Chat now shows an agent selector row when the connected gateway reports multiple agents. Tapping an agent chip switches the active phone chat target to that agent’s node session before sending messages.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[open Android Chat] -> [session/thread chips only] -> [no direct agent choice]

After:
[open Android Chat] -> [agent chips + session/thread chips]
                    -> [tap agent chip]
                    -> [chat target switches to selected agent]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux gateway host; Android physical device
- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw gateway, Android debug APK built from this branch
- Model/provider: multi-agent setup with `main` and local Ollama-backed agent
- Integration/channel (if any): Android node app over gateway connection
- Relevant config (redacted): gateway configured with multiple agents; sensitive tokens/IPs omitted

### Steps

1. Build the Android debug APK from this branch.
2. Install/sideload the APK on an Android phone connected to an OpenClaw gateway with multiple agents configured.
3. Open the Android app Chat surface.
4. Confirm agent chips appear above session/thread chips.
5. Tap a non-default agent chip and send a message.

### Expected

- Android Chat provides a visible way to select the desired gateway agent.
- Selecting an agent switches the active phone chat target to that agent.
- Existing session/thread selection still works below the agent selector.

### Actual

- Agent chips render above session/thread chips when multiple agents are available.
- Selecting another agent switches the chat target and messages route to that agent.
- Existing chat session controls remain available.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [x] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)
![Screenshot_20260510-154513.png](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0637284d-e61c-41b8-a0bb-edf635a94893)


Copied live output from the patched branch:

```text
> Task :app:ktlintCheck

BUILD SUCCESSFUL in 8s
13 actionable tasks: 2 executed, 11 up-to-date
```

```text
> Task :app:assemblePlayDebug

BUILD SUCCESSFUL in 1s
38 actionable tasks: 4 executed, 34 up-to-date
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
  - Android debug APK built successfully from this branch.
  - Android Chat agent selector appeared in a real multi-agent setup.
  - Selecting the alternate agent switched the active chat target before sending.
- Edge cases checked:
  - Selector is hidden when there is only one or zero reported agents.
  - Existing session/thread selector remains available below the new agent selector.
- What you did **not** verify:
  - Full Android instrumentation/E2E test suite.
  - Every possible agent naming/emoji combination.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: the extra chip row could add vertical density to the Chat sheet on small screens.
 - Mitigation: the row uses the same compact horizontal-scroll chip pattern as the existing session selector and is hidden unless multiple agents are available.
- Risk: selecting an agent could be confused with selecting a session/thread.
 - Mitigation: the agent selector is rendered as a separate row above the existing session chips and uses the gateway agent name/emoji where available.

target.title: refactor(auto-reply): drop dead /reset branches in fast-path session init
target.body:
## Summary

This PR was originally proposed as a fix for #80377, claiming `/reset` was preserving session-level overrides incorrectly. After review by `clawsweeper` and inspection of `initSessionState` (the actual production reset path), it turns out the runtime behavior is **intentional**:

- behavior overrides (thinking, verbose, reasoning, ttsAuto) are deliberately preserved across `/reset` so users don't re-enable them every time (see `src/auto-reply/reply/session.ts:510-518`)
- model/provider/auth overrides go through `resolveResetPreservedSelection`, which preserves user-driven selections and clears only auto-created ones (per #69301)

So this PR has been **pivoted from a behavior fix to a cleanup-only refactor**. The original commit (`1a1685a`) and the regression tests it added have been reverted in `1d70235`. The only remaining net change versus `origin/main` is replacing nine trivial `resetTriggered ? existingEntry?.X : existingEntry?.X` ternaries in `initFastReplySessionState` with the simple `existingEntry?.X` expression.

**Net effect: zero semantic change vs `origin/main`.** The dead `resetTriggered` branches no longer mislead readers into thinking the fast-path bootstrap has reset-specific logic that the runtime doesn't actually have.

Issue #80377 should be closed as wontfix / by-design (the bundle pattern that was reported is a copy-paste artifact in this fast-path init, not a production runtime bug). This PR does **not** fix #80377; it only references it.

## Files touched

- `src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply-fast-path.ts` — simplify 9 no-op ternaries (no semantic change)

## Behavior proof (no-op refactor)

This is a semantic no-op cleanup; the proof is the diff itself. Before/after, every assignment evaluates to the same value:

```ts
// Before (one of nine identical patterns)
thinkingLevel: resetTriggered ? existingEntry?.thinkingLevel : existingEntry?.thinkingLevel,
// After
thinkingLevel: existingEntry?.thinkingLevel,
```

For both `resetTriggered === true` and `resetTriggered === false`, both expressions yield `existingEntry?.thinkingLevel`. The `resetTriggered` semantics still flow through the `...(!resetTriggered ? existingEntry : undefined)` spread on line 245 (unchanged), so reset still drops `responseUsage` and `sessionStartedAt` correctly. The full pre-existing test suite for `get-reply.fast-path.test.ts` (12 tests) continues to pass:

```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  12 passed (12)
```

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/auto-reply/reply/get-reply.fast-path.test.ts` → 12/12 (pre-existing tests, unchanged)
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core` → green
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core:test` → green (with `GOMEMLIMIT=2GiB` on a memory-constrained dev box)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: acp: cancel queued SessionActorQueue items on caller abort
target.body:
This PR is narrower than #52747 and targets a different failure mode:
it makes already-enqueued `SessionActorQueue` items cancellable on
caller abort, so `pendingBySession` does not stay inflated when the
predecessor tail never settles. Complementary to #52747's lane task
timeout work and #80239's Pi abort cleanup; does not add any config
or runtime timeout behavior.

## Summary

When a caller's `AbortSignal` fires before its actor reaches the head
of the per-key queue:

- the queued slot is marked cancelled (per-call closure flag),
- `pendingBySession` is decremented eagerly (idempotent, exactly once),
- the wrapped task throws `AbortError` instead of running `op()` when
  its turn arrives,
- per-key serialization invariant is preserved (later same-key tasks
  still wait for predecessor).

`withSessionActor` now forwards its existing signal to
`actorQueue.run(actorKey, op, { signal })` and the manager-side
abort-handling is simplified. The existing diagnostic log line
(`acp-manager: actor queue abort-before-start ...`) is preserved.

## Why

Without caller-side cancellation, `withSessionActor` could reject the
caller's promise on abort-before-start but had no way to remove or
cancel the already-enqueued item. If the predecessor tail did not
settle, the queued item could not reach `finally(onSettle)`, so
`pendingBySession` could not decrement and liveness diagnostics
reported `queued=N last=run:completed` for the actor key
indefinitely.

Sandbox repro reproduces the stale state in a few lines using a
non-settling first task, a same-key enqueue, an abort-before-start
on the second caller, and a measurement of `pendingBySession`.

## What changed

- `src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.ts`: `run()` accepts an
  optional `signal: AbortSignal`. Per-call closure tracks `cancelled`,
  `actorStarted`, `pendingDecremented` flags. Signal listener
  decrements pending eagerly when actor has not started; the wrapped
  task throws `AbortError` when the cancelled slot reaches the queue
  head. Public Plugin SDK (`src/plugin-sdk/keyed-async-queue.ts`) is
  not changed — cancellation is implemented one level up.
- `src/acp/control-plane/manager.core.ts`: `withSessionActor` passes
  its existing signal to `actorQueue.run(actorKey, op, { signal })`.
  Manager-side `markAbortBeforeStart` call and the inline abort
  listener are no longer needed on this path; the queue takes over.
  Diagnostic log preserved verbatim.
- `src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.test.ts`: 7 new tests
  covering signal-based cancellation, eager pending decrement,
  idempotent decrement, per-key serialization preservation,
  pre-aborted signal, queue still alive after cancelled item,
  backwards-compatibility for callers without signal. Existing
  diagnostic test preserved.

## Verification

pnpm test src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.test.ts
✓ 8 passed

pnpm test src/acp/control-plane/session-actor-queue.test.ts
          src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts
✓ 64 passed

pnpm check:changed       ✓ EXIT 0
pnpm build               ✓ EXIT 0
pnpm exec oxfmt --check  ✓ clean

Live runtime validation on macOS gateway:
- Built from cherry-pick onto `v2026.5.6` tag
- Restarted gateway with patched `dist/` (smoke run 4.6s, status ok)
- Patched bundle contains `Aborted before start`, `pendingDecremented`,
  `actor queue abort-before-start` markers — fix loaded in memory
- 24h passive monitor observed no new
  `queued=N last=run:completed` stale liveness warnings

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Caller abort-before-start for already-enqueued `SessionActorQueue` items no longer leaves `pendingBySession` inflated when the predecessor tail never settles.
- **Real environment tested:** macOS live OpenClaw gateway setup, patched npm install built from cherry-pick onto `v2026.5.6`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Cherry-picked the source fix onto `v2026.5.6`, built, rsynced patched `dist/` into the live npm install, restarted the gateway, then ran a live smoke request against the restarted gateway process.
- **Evidence after fix:** Live output/runtime evidence from the patched setup: gateway restarted with pid `18313`; smoke run completed in `4.6s` with `status ok`; no Codex schema errors were observed. Patched bundle `dist/manager-D7F0BwP2.js` contains the after-fix markers `Aborted before start`, `pendingDecremented`, and `actor queue abort-before-start`. A 24h passive monitor is still running and has observed `0` stale queue warnings so far.
- **Observed result after fix:** The patched live gateway stayed up after restart, the smoke request succeeded, the patched queue cancellation markers were loaded in the running bundle, and passive monitoring has not seen new `queued=N last=run:completed` stale liveness warnings.
- **What was not tested:** Cross-OS live gateway validation was not repeated for this PR; local validation covered tests, changed checks, build, and formatter.

## Notes for reviewers

- This is intentionally separate from #52747 because the failure
  modes differ: #52747 targets a lane task that has *started but is
  stuck*, while this PR targets a caller cancellation *before the
  actor starts*. The two are complementary; either can be merged
  without conflicting with the other's intent, though both touch
  `session-actor-queue.ts` and `manager.core.ts` and will require a
  rebase of the second-merged PR.
- Public Plugin SDK is intentionally untouched. Backwards
  compatibility: `run(actorKey, op)` without `options` behaves
  identically to before.
- An earlier deployment experiment confirmed that `dist`-only
  patching across version lines is unsafe (Codex schema + Control
  UI mismatch). The fix in this PR is targeted at the source level
  and rebuilds cleanly on the same release line.
- May address parts of #13744 (session lock timeout) by reducing
  queue pressure that contributes to lock contention, though
  #13744's primary scope is the lock itself.

target.title: feat(discord): add implicit reply mention policy
target.body:
Closes #80234.

Adds `channels.discord.implicitReplyMentions`:

```json5
{
  fromUsers: true,
  fromBots: true
}
```

Defaults preserve current behavior. Multi-bot deployments can set `fromBots: false` so bot-authored native replies do not wake another bot via the implicit `reply_to_bot` path; explicit `<@botId>` mentions still work.

Checks run:

- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.preflight.test.ts --maxWorkers=1`
- `pnpm config:schema:check`
- `pnpm config:docs:check`
- `pnpm check:bundled-channel-config-metadata`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: Discord native reply-only mentions can wake an OpenClaw bot when another bot replies to one of its messages. This PR adds a policy knob so deployments can keep human reply activation while disabling bot-authored reply-only activation.

Real environment tested: Private Discord server with a private test channel and two OpenClaw Discord bot accounts. Server, channel, bot names, message IDs, and account IDs are intentionally omitted from this public proof.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
1. In the private test channel, send an anchor message from the target bot.
2. Send a native Discord reply from a separate relay bot to that anchor message. The reply body contains no textual `<@bot>` mention.
3. Observe whether the target bot wakes/responds.
4. Repeat after applying this branch with `implicitReplyMentions: { fromUsers: true, fromBots: false }`.
5. Send a separate control message from the relay bot that contains a real textual `<@bot>` mention and asks the target bot to reply with a known marker.

Evidence after fix: Redacted live output excerpt from the private Discord test channel:

```text
after-fix native reply-only test:
relay bot | "oc-proof-80235-after4-231018 native-reply-no-explicit-mention" | ref=<target-bot-anchor>
UNEXPECTED_TARGET_RESPONSES=0

explicit mention control:
target bot | "oc-proof-80235-control-231107 explicit-mention-ok" | ref=<relay-bot-control-message>
EXPLICIT_CONTROL_OK=true
```

Observed result after fix: With `fromBots:false`, the bot-authored native reply-only message did not wake the target bot during the observation window. A real textual explicit mention still woke the target bot and produced the requested marker response.

What was not tested: Human-authored reply-only behavior was covered by unit tests but not repeated in the live Discord proof. PluralKit behavior was covered by unit tests but not repeated in the live Discord proof.

target.title: [codex] Add Codex continuity bridge
target.body:
## Summary

Adds the Codex Continuity Bridge to the bundled Codex plugin.

The bridge keeps Codex UI as the high-bandwidth workbench while giving OpenClaw a quiet operator surface for Telegram: status, recent threads, explicit watched completion checks, evidence-separated handoff briefs, and a gated write path that is disabled by default.

## Safety Model

- Read-only Codex app-server status is preferred.
- SQLite fallback is read-only and explicitly stale-labeled.
- Raw Codex app-server blobs are not exposed by the public HTTP routes.
- Watch notifications are explicit, deduped, redacted, and quiet by default.
- Telegram-to-Codex writes require an explicit feature flag plus provenance, trusted sender checks, repo allowlisting, risk gating, unambiguous target selection, and confirmed app-server write methods.
- SQLite is never used as a write path.

## Validation

- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm exec tsc -p extensions/codex/tsconfig.json --noEmit`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/codex/src/continuity/bridge.test.ts extensions/codex/src/commands.test.ts` (89 tests)
- Local gateway smoke test for `/codex/status`, `/codex/threads`, `/codex/watch`, `/codex/watch/check`, `/codex/handoff`, and `/codex/goal`

target.title: fix(acp): reject fractional runtime timeouts
target.body:
## Summary
- reject fractional numeric ACP runtime timeout patches instead of silently rounding them
- add regression coverage for fractional `timeoutSeconds` values

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** ACP runtime option validation should reject fractional numeric `timeoutSeconds` values instead of silently rounding them. Integer seconds should still be accepted.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS after `pnpm install`, branch `fix/acp-runtime-timeout-integer`, commit `571ad9f7d6`.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a live Node command with `tsx` import support against the local checkout; called `validateRuntimeOptionPatch` with `1.4`, `1.6`, and integer `2`.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the live command:

```console
$ node --import tsx -e "import { validateRuntimeOptionPatch } from './src/acp/control-plane/runtime-options.ts'; for (const value of [1.4, 1.6]) { try { console.log(value, validateRuntimeOptionPatch({ timeoutSeconds: value })); } catch (error) { console.log(value, error instanceof Error ? error.message : String(error)); } } console.log('integer', JSON.stringify(validateRuntimeOptionPatch({ timeoutSeconds: 2 })));"
1.4 Timeout must be a positive integer in seconds.
1.6 Timeout must be a positive integer in seconds.
integer {"timeoutSeconds":2}
```

**Observed result after fix:** Fractional numeric timeout patches now fail with `Timeout must be a positive integer in seconds.`, while an integer timeout patch still returns `{ "timeoutSeconds": 2 }`.

**What was not tested:** Full live ACP client session. The proof exercises the actual runtime-options module in a real local OpenClaw checkout; unit and changed-scope checks cover the integration surface.

## Test Plan
- `pnpm test src/acp/control-plane/runtime-options.test.ts src/acp/control-plane/manager.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Notes
The string/config-option path already requires integer seconds via `parseRuntimeTimeoutSecondsInput`; this aligns the numeric runtime patch path with that contract.

target.title: fix(matrix): bound raw transport response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes unbounded raw body buffering in Matrix SDK transport when `maxBytes` is not provided by the caller.

**Root cause:** `performMatrixRequest` with `raw: true` fell through to `Buffer.from(await response.arrayBuffer())` when `params.maxBytes` was undefined. Any caller that omitted `maxBytes` (for example `downloadContent(mxcUrl)` in `sdk.ts`) could buffer arbitrarily large media files — images, videos, encrypted attachments — exhausting process memory.

**Fix:** Use the existing `MATRIX_SDK_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES` constant as the safe default upper bound. All raw reads now go through `readResponseWithLimit`.

**Compatibility:** Callers that already pass `maxBytes` see no behavior change. Callers that omit it now get `MatrixMediaSizeLimitError` if the response exceeds `MATRIX_SDK_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES`. This is fail-closed: the alternative was unbounded OOM.

## Evidence

Focused local validation after the lint follow-up:

```text
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts -- --run

Test Files  1 passed (1)
     Tests  16 passed (16)
```

Focused lint validation after the follow-up:

```text
oxlint extensions/matrix/src/matrix/sdk/transport.test.ts

(no findings)
```

The test suite includes two real `node:http` server proofs bound to `127.0.0.1` with no runtime fetch stub override:

- Over-cap proof: server declares `Content-Length: MATRIX_SDK_RESPONSE_MAX_BYTES + 1` with `maxBytes` omitted. `enforceDeclaredResponseSize` rejects before buffering the body and throws `MatrixMediaSizeLimitError`.
- Under-cap proof: server sends a small payload with `maxBytes` omitted. The transport applies the default cap and returns the raw `Buffer` normally.

These prove both the fail-closed oversized path and the compatibility path for normal raw responses through the real HTTP transport path.

## Security & Privacy

No new network destinations. No credential handling. This narrows a memory-exhaustion path: responses that previously buffered without limit now reject at the Matrix SDK default raw response cap.

## Compatibility

| Scenario | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| `downloadContent(url)` with large media and no explicit `maxBytes` | OOM / process crash risk | Fail-closed `MatrixMediaSizeLimitError` |
| `downloadContent(url)` with normal media and no explicit `maxBytes` | Returns full Buffer | Returns full Buffer |
| Caller provides explicit `maxBytes` | Bounded by caller value | Bounded by caller value |

target.title: fix: render workspace avatars in identity card
target.body:
﻿Closes #97602

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where workspace-relative agent avatars could appear broken in the Control UI Personal card because identity/bootstrap payloads could hand the browser an auth-gated `/avatar/<agentId>` URL that plain image elements cannot load with Bearer auth.

## Why This Change Was Made

Avatar URL projection now lives with the existing agent avatar owner code in `src/agents/identity-avatar.ts` instead of duplicating workspace file handling in gateway session projection. `agent.identity.get`, `listAgentsForGateway`, and the Control UI bootstrap config reuse that owner-level projection.

The shared local-file projection keeps the workspace file-read boundary on `openRootFileSync` and carries the resolved workspace root with local avatar resolutions, so data URI reads still happen through the root-safe opened-fd path. Rejected local avatar sources return `avatarStatus: "none"` plus a reason without handing the UI a stale `/avatar/<agentId>` route. Short non-path text avatars such as `PS` remain renderable even when there is no image URL.

Unsupported local avatar extensions are intentionally blocked by the existing local avatar policy. The supported local avatar set is image-only (`png`, `jpg/jpeg`, `gif`, `webp`, `svg`); files such as `notes.txt` are reported as `unsupported_extension` and are not inlined.

AI-assisted with Codex.

## User Impact

Control UI can render valid workspace-relative Personal card avatars consistently across bootstrap, identity refresh, agent list, and chat-adjacent identity surfaces, without making unauthenticated `/avatar/<agentId>` image requests. Missing, outside-workspace, too-large, or unsupported local avatar image files fall back to the product logo/text presentation and keep their source/status/reason visible for repair. Existing short text avatar fallbacks remain visible.

## Evidence

- Identity RPC and symlink boundary coverage: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts -- -t "agent.identity.get|symlinks that escape"` -> 10 passed, 326 skipped
- Bootstrap config coverage: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/control-ui.http.test.ts -- -t "bootstrap config"` -> 44 passed, 192 skipped
- Avatar projection/list coverage: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts -- -t "avatar"` -> 8 passed, 424 skipped
- UI render coverage, including rejected-route suppression and short text avatar preservation: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/views/config-quick.test.ts ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts` -> 39 passed
- Whitespace check: `git diff --check`

Additional red/green checks run while building this PR:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts -- -t "returns workspace-relative avatars as data URLs"`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts -- -t "does not inline unsupported workspace avatar files"`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts -- -t "keeps identity data URLs renderable"`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/app-render.assistant-avatar.test.ts -- -t "does not render stale route avatars"`

Notes:
- Full `src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts` on this Windows checkout still has unrelated pre-existing failures around SQLite temp cleanup `EBUSY` and `/tmp` path normalization, so focused avatar coverage was used for this PR.
- Local autoreview was attempted after the final refactor, but the autoreview wrapper failed before engine review with `WinError 267` while resolving `git` from this Windows checkout path. Earlier post-fix autoreview also hit the Codex account usage limit, so there is no successful fresh autoreview result to claim.

target.title: fix(browser): time out remote tab enumeration
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #58968 by bounding remote CDP Playwright tab enumeration with the existing remote CDP timeout budget.
- Evicts the scoped cached/in-flight Playwright connection on enumeration timeout so the next request can reconnect instead of reusing a stuck pipe.
- Adds regression coverage for a stuck page-target lookup and verifies remote profile tab listing passes the resolved timeout into the Playwright path.

## Problem
Remote CDP profiles use a persistent Playwright connection for tab listing because HTTP `/json/list` is ephemeral for those providers. When that Playwright-backed enumeration stalls, browser requests can hang behind the stuck CDP operation instead of failing fast.

## Root Cause
`createProfileTabOps().readTabs()` passed only `cdpUrl` and SSRF policy to `listPagesViaPlaywright()`. The lower-level Playwright enumeration path had no operation-level timeout around `connectBrowser()`, `getAllPages()`, or per-page target/title/url reads, and a stuck scoped connection could remain cached or in-flight.

## Architectural Reasoning
The fix reuses the existing browser remote CDP timeout contract rather than adding a new config key. Remote tab listing now uses `max(remoteCdpTimeoutMs, remoteCdpHandshakeTimeoutMs)`, which matches the existing distinction between HTTP discovery and WebSocket handshake budgets. Timeout cleanup goes through the existing scoped Playwright disconnect helper, preserving per-CDP URL isolation and avoiding hidden coupling to other browser profiles.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Remote CDP Playwright tab enumeration now returns a bounded timeout error instead of waiting indefinitely on a stalled CDP discovery/enumeration path.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 worktree, Node `v22.15.0`, `pnpm 10.33.2`, branch `hemant/issue-triage-20260509`, commit `9aba9b405ae5c4601211fd0b2b428d9640425b8b`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Started a real loopback HTTP endpoint that accepts CDP discovery connections and deliberately never responds; called production `listPagesViaPlaywright()` through `node --import tsx` with `timeoutMs: 100` and an SSRF policy allowing loopback/private access; closed the scoped Playwright connection and destroyed the held socket after the call.
- **Evidence after fix:**

```console
$ node --import tsx - < hanging-cdp-smoke.mjs
{"ok":true,"error":"Playwright page enumeration timed out after 100ms","elapsedMs":309}
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The production browser module returned `Playwright page enumeration timed out after 100ms` and the script exited cleanly instead of hanging on the held CDP request.
- **What was not tested:** I did not use a real remote Chrome host because the failure mode is a stalled CDP endpoint; the local loopback endpoint exercises the same production timeout path with deterministic cleanup. Full `pnpm test extensions/browser` still has unrelated Windows environment failures noted below.

## Testing
- `pnpm test extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.connections.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.remote-profile-tab-ops.playwright.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.tab-ops.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/pw-session.connections.test.ts extensions/browser/src/browser/server-context.remote-profile-tab-ops.playwright.test.ts`
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` from `O:\` subst drive to avoid the repo path-with-spaces launcher bug
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` from `O:\`
- `pnpm lint:extensions` from `O:\`
- `pnpm check:changelog-attributions`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`
- `pnpm check:changed --staged` exited 0 with no lane output

Full `pnpm test extensions/browser` was also run locally. It failed on unrelated Windows environment assumptions: local browser auto-detection / missing supported browser launch fixtures and existing `/tmp` path expectations being normalized to `C:\tmp`. The two touched regression test files pass.

## Risk Analysis
Risk is medium because this touches async browser/CDP lifecycle behavior. The change is scoped to remote Playwright tab enumeration, does not alter local managed browser JSON tab listing, and does not change navigation, action, or screenshot behavior. On timeout, OpenClaw drops only the scoped Playwright connection for that CDP URL and leaves the actual remote browser tabs intact.

## Backward Compatibility
No config, API, or response-shape changes. Existing `browser.remoteCdpTimeoutMs` and `browser.remoteCdpHandshakeTimeoutMs` now also bound remote Playwright tab enumeration.

## Screenshots / Logs
The real behavior proof above is terminal output from the after-fix smoke; no UI screenshot is applicable.

target.title: fix(skills): warn for untrusted local sources
target.body:
Fixes #57535

## Problem summary
Local skills from workspace, `.agents`, managed, and extra skill roots were shown as ordinary ready/blocked skills. Operators had no first-class status signal distinguishing ClawHub/bundled skills from local skill files that should be reviewed before enablement or invocation.

## Root cause
The skills loader already tracks each skill source, and ClawHub installs already write `.clawhub/origin.json`, but `buildWorkspaceSkillStatus` collapsed that into `source`/`bundled` only. CLI and Control UI status surfaces therefore had no trust metadata to render.

## Architectural reasoning
This keeps runtime loading semantics unchanged and adds trust classification at the status/reporting layer. Built-in skills are trusted as `openclaw-bundled`; valid ClawHub origin metadata is trusted as `clawhub`; reviewed local roots configured in `skills.load.trustedDirs` are trusted as `trusted-dir`; known local sources without either signal are marked `untrustedLocalSource` with a warning. The change is additive to the status API and avoids coupling core loader behavior to owner-specific skill ids.

## Testing performed
- `pnpm test src/agents/skills-status.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/skills-status.ts src/agents/skills-status.test.ts src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.format.ts src/cli/skills-cli.test.ts src/config/types.skills.ts src/config/zod-schema.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts ui/src/ui/types.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills-shared.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.test.ts docs/tools/skills.md docs/tools/skills-config.md` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed.
- `pnpm lint:docs` passed.
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` passed via a temporary `P:\` mapping to avoid the repo path-with-spaces issue.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` passed via the same temporary mapping.
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui` passed via the same temporary mapping.
- `pnpm config:docs:check` initially detected expected config baseline drift; after `pnpm config:docs:gen`, `pnpm config:docs:check` passed.
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` passed after clean rebase.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed locally. Blacksmith Testbox was unavailable because `blacksmith` is not installed on PATH.

`pnpm test:changed` was also run locally and selected a broad `unit-fast` shard; it failed in 19 unrelated files with existing Windows/path/host assumptions such as `/tmp` becoming `C:\tmp`, POSIX table glyph expectations on Windows output, A2UI scaffold 404 in `extensions/canvas`, and temp SQLite cleanup `EBUSY`. The focused touched-surface tests above passed.

`pnpm format:docs:check` could not be used as proof in this environment because the wrapper crashed on Node v22.15.0 with `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')` in `scripts/format-docs.mjs`; targeted `oxfmt --check`, `lint:docs`, and `docs:check-mdx` passed.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: A temporary workspace skill without ClawHub origin metadata is now classified as an untrusted local source.
- Real environment tested: Windows checkout on branch `hemant/57535-untrusted-skill-sources`, Node v22.15.0, pnpm 10.33.2.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a `pnpm exec tsx` script from the repo root that created a temporary `<workspace>/skills/local-review/SKILL.md`, called `buildWorkspaceSkillStatus(workspace)`, and rendered `formatSkillsCheck(report, {})`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from the local run:

```json
{
  "name": "local-review",
  "source": "openclaw-workspace",
  "trustSource": "local",
  "untrustedLocalSource": true,
  "hasWarning": true
}
```

```text
Untrusted local sources
local-review
Add the directory to skills.load.trustedDirs only if you trust this workspace-owned skill source.
```

- Observed result after fix: Local skill loading still succeeded and the status/check surface warned before users enable or invoke the local skill.
- What was not tested: No browser screenshot was captured because this is a status/API/CLI/UI-rendering change covered by jsdom UI tests rather than a visual layout change.

## Risk analysis
Low-to-medium. The classification is additive and synchronous, using small local origin/trusted-dir checks during status generation. It does not alter skill discovery, precedence, eligibility, model visibility, command visibility, or execution. Main residual risk is users seeing new warnings for legitimate local/plugin-provided skill roots until they add reviewed directories to `skills.load.trustedDirs`.

## Backward compatibility
Backward-compatible. Existing skill config continues to load the same skills. New `SkillStatusEntry` fields are additive, and `skills.load.trustedDirs` is optional. Existing `skills.load.allowSymlinkTargets` behavior is unchanged; docs now clarify that it controls loader containment while `trustedDirs` controls warning suppression.

## Screenshots/logs
No screenshots. Relevant command output is summarized above.

target.title: fix(approvals): summarize long approval prompts
target.body:
## Problem summary

Fixes #58687. Long exec approval prompts can render enough command text that the approval controls become hard to reach or review, especially for inline scripts. The Control UI also rendered an Allow Always button even when the gateway omitted `allow-always` from the request's allowed decisions, making that action look ineffective.

## Root cause

Exec approval request payloads were already sanitized/redacted before display, but presentation surfaces still rendered the whole sanitized command up to the existing display cap. The Control UI parser also ignored `request.allowedDecisions`, so it could offer a decision that the gateway would reject for `ask: "always"` or other restricted approval modes.

## Architectural reasoning

- Adds a browser-safe command prompt summarizer in `src/infra` and uses it only at presentation boundaries: fallback forwarded approval text, shared approval reply payloads, native approval view models, and the Control UI modal.
- Leaves the pending approval record, sanitized command payload, `systemRunPlan`, bindings, and resolution path unchanged, so command identity and allow-always matching are not loosened.
- Preserves short command rendering exactly and only summarizes when the prompt exceeds five logical lines or 1,200 display chars.
- Teaches the Control UI approval parser to carry `allowedDecisions` for exec and plugin approval requests, then renders only those actions in the modal.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: long exec approval command text is summarized before prompt rendering, and Control UI approval parsing preserves the gateway-provided allowed decisions so unavailable Allow Always choices can be omitted.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Windows, Node v22.15.0, pnpm 10.33.2, branch `hemant/58687-approval-prompt-summary` at `aaa32699bfff52d4147d2850f456eaa2c88f3dbe`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Imported the actual runtime helper from `src/infra/exec-approval-command-summary.ts` with `pnpm exec tsx`.
  2. Built an eight-line approval command using the same escaped line-break marker shape that sanitized approval command text can contain.
  3. Parsed a synthetic gateway `exec.approval.requested` payload through the actual Control UI parser with `allowedDecisions: ["allow-once", "deny"]`.
  4. Printed the resulting prompt summary and parsed decisions.
- Evidence after fix:
```console
$ pnpm exec tsx -e "import { summarizeExecApprovalCommandForPrompt } from './src/infra/exec-approval-command-summary.ts'; import { parseExecApprovalRequested } from './ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.ts'; const command = Array.from({ length: 8 }, (_, i) => 'echo line ' + (i + 1) + '\\u{A}').join(''); const summary = summarizeExecApprovalCommandForPrompt(command); const parsed = parseExecApprovalRequested({ id: 'proof-1', request: { command: 'echo ok', allowedDecisions: ['allow-once', 'deny'] }, createdAtMs: 1, expiresAtMs: 2 }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ summaryTruncated: summary.truncated, hiddenLineCount: summary.hiddenLineCount, includesLine8: summary.text.includes('line 8'), summaryPreview: summary.text.split('\n').slice(0, 6), parsedAllowedDecisions: parsed?.request.allowedDecisions }, null, 2));"
{
  "summaryTruncated": true,
  "hiddenLineCount": 4,
  "includesLine8": false,
  "summaryPreview": [
    "echo line 1\\u{A}",
    "echo line 2\\u{A}",
    "echo line 3\\u{A}",
    "echo line 4\\u{A}",
    "echo line 5\\u{A}",
    "...[truncated: showing first 5 of 9 lines; 52 chars hidden]"
  ],
  "parsedAllowedDecisions": [
    "allow-once",
    "deny"
  ]
}
```
- Observed result after fix: the prompt summary is truncated, hides the tail (`includesLine8: false`), reports the hidden content, and the parser returns only `allow-once` and `deny`, which is the decision set the modal will render.
- What was not tested: I did not attach a screenshot because the browser modal path is covered by `ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.browser.test.ts`; I did not run a live gateway approval through an external chat account because the changed code is presentation-only and the native adapter tests cover Matrix, Slack, and Telegram rendering.

## Testing performed

- `pnpm test src/infra/exec-approval-command-summary.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts src/infra/approval-view-model.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.browser.test.ts src/infra/approval-handler-runtime.test.ts extensions/matrix/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/slack/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md docs/tools/exec-approvals-advanced.md src/infra/exec-approval-command-summary.ts src/infra/exec-approval-command-summary.test.ts src/infra/approval-view-model.ts src/infra/approval-view-model.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.ts src/infra/exec-approval-forwarder.test.ts src/infra/exec-approval-reply.ts src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/exec-approval.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.ts ui/src/ui/views/exec-approval.test.ts`
- `pnpm lint:core`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `pnpm lint:docs`
- `pnpm tsgo:core` via temporary `P:\` mapping because direct execution from `C:\Projects\Personal Project\openclaw` still trips the path-with-spaces launcher issue
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` via temporary `P:\` mapping
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui` via temporary `P:\` mapping
- `pnpm check:changed`
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD`

`pnpm test:changed` fanned out to the broad `unit-fast` shard and failed on unrelated Windows/local-environment assumptions: POSIX path expectation mismatches, table glyph fallback expectations, SQLite temp cleanup `EBUSY`, missing A2UI scaffold returning 404, and Testbox local-key fixture assumptions. The focused approval, UI, and native adapter tests above passed.

## Risk analysis

Low-to-medium. The changed behavior is intentionally display-only except for honoring already-present `allowedDecisions` in the Control UI. Approval matching, pending record storage, node/gateway resolution, command binding, and execution semantics are unchanged. The main risk is that native approval clients now receive summarized `view.commandText` for long commands, covered by native handler tests across Matrix, Slack, and Telegram.

## Screenshots/logs

No screenshot was needed; the browser approval modal regression test covers the rendered modal path. The real behavior proof and test command summaries above are the relevant logs.

## Backward compatibility

Existing approval request payload shape is compatible. Clients that do not send `allowedDecisions` still see the historical default actions. Short command prompts render unchanged; long prompts are summarized only in human-facing approval surfaces while the full sanitized command remains bound to the approval record.

The PR adds generated and documented systemd `WatchdogSec`/`NotifyAccess` settings, a `systemd-notify` helper wired to the gateway maintenance tick, focused tests, docs, and a changelog entry.

PR surface: Source +95, Tests +98, Docs +9. Total +202 across 10 files.

Reproducibility: no. live high-confidence reproduction of the original hung-gateway issue was established. The PR defect is source/dependency-reproducible: systemd expects `WATCHDOG=1`, while the PR sends unsupported `systemd-notify --watchdog`.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Human review is needed because the remaining blockers are target-system proof, default-supervision product/upgrade judgment, and conflict refresh rather than a safe standalone automation repair.

target.title: fix(gateway): add systemd watchdog heartbeat
target.body:
## Problem summary

Closes #11973. The generated systemd gateway unit had crash restart behavior, but it did not configure a watchdog heartbeat. If the gateway event loop wedged without exiting, systemd had no liveness signal to trigger recovery.

## Root cause

The existing gateway maintenance loop already emits a 30s keepalive tick, but that liveness signal only reaches OpenClaw clients. The systemd unit renderer did not opt into `WatchdogSec`, and the gateway runtime did not notify systemd through the `NOTIFY_SOCKET`/`systemd-notify` contract.

## Architectural reasoning

- Added a small `src/infra/systemd-notify.ts` helper that is inert unless `NOTIFY_SOCKET` is present, so non-systemd, Windows, macOS, foreground, and unmanaged gateway runs keep the same behavior.
- Reused `startGatewayMaintenanceTimers` as the watchdog cadence source. This ties the systemd ping to the existing event-loop tick instead of creating a separate interval or lifecycle path.
- Kept the systemd unit on the existing default `Type=simple` behavior and only added `WatchdogSec=90s` plus `NotifyAccess=all`. The `NotifyAccess=all` line is needed because notifications are sent by the `systemd-notify` helper process inside the service cgroup.
- The notifier disables itself after the first `systemd-notify` failure and logs once, avoiding repeated subprocess failures while preserving the gateway's existing session, cleanup, and health loops.
- No gateway protocol, API, session, or backward-compatibility contract changes.

## Testing performed

- `pnpm test src/infra/systemd-notify.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts` passed after rebase: 3 Vitest shards, 19 tests.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/systemd-notify.ts src/infra/systemd-notify.test.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.ts src/gateway/server-maintenance.test.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts docs/gateway/index.md docs/platforms/linux.md docs/vps.md CHANGELOG.md` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed.
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx` passed.
- `pnpm lint:docs -- docs/gateway/index.md docs/platforms/linux.md docs/vps.md` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm tsgo:core:test` passed from a subst drive path to avoid the local Windows path-with-space issue.
- `pnpm check:changed --staged` and `pnpm check:changed` exited 0 in this checkout, though they produced no lane output.

Known local environment limitations:

- Adding `src/daemon/systemd.test.ts` to the focused test command still shows pre-existing Windows-only failures unrelated to this change: `process.geteuid` is absent on Windows, and two env-file assertions expect an unquoted Windows path. The changed renderer test itself passes.
- `pnpm build` could not be completed in this Windows shell. The default `pnpm` wrapper is pinned to local Node v22.15.0 while the repo requires `>=22.16.0`; a subst-drive retry with bundled Node v24.14.0 gets past that but `build-all` still fails at the bootstrap guard because the expected generated `.js` dist aliases are missing in this environment. A direct `tsdown` build completed successfully, and generated hash drift was restored.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated systemd gateway units now include watchdog recovery settings, and the runtime notifier is a safe no-op outside systemd-managed services.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows, branch `hemant/11973-systemd-watchdog`, Node v22.15.0 for focused proof commands.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `pnpm exec tsx` commands from the repo root to render the generated systemd unit and invoke the notifier with no `NOTIFY_SOCKET`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from the local run:

```console
$ pnpm exec tsx -e "import { buildSystemdUnit } from './src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts'; const unit = buildSystemdUnit({ description: 'OpenClaw Gateway', programArguments: ['/usr/bin/openclaw','gateway','run'], environment: {} }); console.log(unit.split('\n').filter((line) => ['Restart=always','RestartSec=5','WatchdogSec=90s','NotifyAccess=all','KillMode=control-group'].includes(line)).join('\n'));"
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
WatchdogSec=90s
NotifyAccess=all
KillMode=control-group

$ pnpm exec tsx -e "import { createSystemdNotifier } from './src/infra/systemd-notify.ts'; void (async () => { const notifier = createSystemdNotifier({ env: {} }); await notifier.ready(); await notifier.watchdog(); console.log('systemd notifier no-op without NOTIFY_SOCKET ok'); })();"
systemd notifier no-op without NOTIFY_SOCKET ok
```

- Observed result after fix: The actual unit renderer includes the watchdog lines, and the notifier performs no work when the systemd notification socket is absent. Unmanaged/local gateway runs remain unaffected, while managed systemd units can receive liveness pings from the existing maintenance tick.
- What was not tested: A live Linux systemd user service restart on a hung event loop from this Windows workspace.

## Risk analysis

Medium-low. The unit settings affect systemd-managed gateway installs only. Runtime notifications are gated by `NOTIFY_SOCKET`, failure-disabled after the first subprocess error, and do not change gateway protocol/session flows. The main operational risk is a missing `systemd-notify` binary on a systemd host; in that case the helper logs once and leaves existing crash-restart behavior intact.

## Screenshots/logs

No UI screenshots. This is CLI/service generation behavior; relevant command output is included above.

## Backward compatibility

Backward compatible. Existing unmanaged gateway runs, existing installed units until regenerated, non-systemd platforms, gateway API behavior, and session/gateway protocol behavior are unchanged. Docs now show the watchdog lines alongside the generated unit settings.
