target.title: feat(docker): support OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS env var
target.body:
Closes #93142

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where operators deploying OpenClaw via Portainer or other GUI-managed Compose stacks could not configure `gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins` without running an extra shell command. The Docker setup script already auto-seeds localhost loopback origins for non-loopback binds, but did not accept user-provided non-localhost origins, forcing operators to run `docker compose run ... config set --batch-json` manually after every deployment.

## Why This Change Was Made

Adds `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS` environment variable to `scripts/docker/setup.sh`. When set, the env var value (a JSON array of origin strings) is validated and passed to `config set --batch-json` in `sync_gateway_config`, persisting it into `openclaw.json` — the same mechanism already used for `gateway.bind` and the default localhost origins.

Design decisions:

✅ Portainer users running `setup.sh` can now configure non-localhost origins via env var — the primary target use case
✅ Persisted to `openclaw.json` via existing `config set` pipeline, keeping origin allowlist in durable config storage
✅ Security: env var consumed only at setup time, not at runtime; resulting allowlist is file-auditable in `openclaw.json`
✅ Follows the same script-layer propagation pattern as `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_BIND`
✅ Shell-level validation (`validate_allowed_origins_value`) rejects malformed env values at setup time, preventing batch JSON structure corruption
❌ Does not cover users who skip `setup.sh` entirely — the existing manual flow (`docs/install/docker.md:128`) already documents `config set --batch-json` for that path and can be updated separately with the env var

No TS core changes needed.

## User Impact

Portainer and Docker Compose users can now set `OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS='["https://openclaw.example.com"]'` in their environment and have it automatically applied on setup, without needing shell access for `config set` commands.

## Evidence

### Unit / E2E tests (42 passed)

```
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  42 passed (42)
```

All 42 tests pass, including 2 new tests:
- `honors OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS env var override` — valid custom origin is written to config
- `rejects OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS with non-array value` — malformed value is rejected at setup time

### Real Docker proof

Test results from an offline Docker deployment (pre-loaded `ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest`, `setup.sh --offline`):

- Custom origin (`http://my-vm-host:18789`) is written to config and persisted
- Control UI health endpoint is reachable
- WebSocket `connect` with matched origin → origin allowed (proceeds to device identity check)
- WebSocket `connect` with unlisted origin (`https://evil.com`) → `ORIGIN_NOT_ALLOWED` error
- WebSocket `connect` without origin → `ORIGIN_NOT_ALLOWED` (`origin missing or invalid`)
- Default fallback (`localhost` + `127.0.0.1`) when env var is unset
- Malformed value (`not-a-json-array`) is rejected with a clear error; gateway does not start

<details>
<summary>Initial proof: setup persistence, health check, default fallback, malformed value rejection</summary>

```

Step 1: Custom origin setup

[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# export OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS='["http://my-vm-host:18789"]'
[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# export OPENCLAW_SKIP_ONBOARDING=1
[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# ./scripts/docker/setup.sh --offline
Reusing gateway token from /data/openclaw/.env
==> Using preloaded Docker image: ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest

==> Fixing data-directory permissions
[+] Creating 1/1
 ✔ Network openclaw_default  Created                                                                                                                                      0.1s 

==> Skipping onboarding (OPENCLAW_SKIP_ONBOARDING is set)

==> Docker gateway defaults
Pinned gateway.mode=local and gateway.bind=lan for Docker setup.
Set gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins to ["http://my-vm-host:18789"] for non-loopback bind.

==> Provider setup (optional)
WhatsApp (QR):
  docker compose -f /data/openclaw/docker-compose.yml run --rm openclaw-cli channels login
Telegram (bot token):
  docker compose -f /data/openclaw/docker-compose.yml run --rm openclaw-cli channels add --channel telegram --token <token>
Discord (bot token):
  docker compose -f /data/openclaw/docker-compose.yml run --rm openclaw-cli channels add --channel discord --token <token>
Docs: https://docs.openclaw.ai/channels

==> Starting gateway
[+] Running 1/1
 ✔ Container openclaw-openclaw-gateway-1  Started                                                                                                                         0.4s 
[+] Creating 1/1
 ✔ Container openclaw-openclaw-gateway-1  Running0.0s 

Gateway running with host port mapping.
Access from tailnet devices via the host's tailnet IP.
Config: /root/.openclaw
Workspace: /root/.openclaw/workspace
Token: stored in Docker environment/config (not printed).

Commands:
  docker compose -f /data/openclaw/docker-compose.yml logs -f openclaw-gateway
  docker compose -f /data/openclaw/docker-compose.yml exec openclaw-gateway sh -lc 'node dist/index.js health --token "$OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_TOKEN"'


Step 2: Persisted in config

[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# docker compose run --rm openclaw-cli config get gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins
[+] Creating 1/1
 ✔ Container openclaw-openclaw-gateway-1  Running                                                                                                                           0.0s 

OpenClaw 2026.6.10 (unknown) — I'm the assistant your terminal demanded, not the one your sleep schedule requested.

[
  "http://my-vm-host:18789"
]

Step 3: Control UI reachable

[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# curl -s -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" http://127.0.0.1:18789/healthz
HTTP 200
[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# curl -s -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" http://my-vm-host:18789/healthz
HTTP 200
[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# curl -s -H "Origin: http://my-vm-host:18789" -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" http://127.0.0.1:18789/healthz
HTTP 200
[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# curl -s -H "Origin: https://evil.com" -o /dev/null -w "HTTP %{http_code}\n" http://127.0.0.1:18789/healthz
HTTP 200


Step 4: Default fallback (unset env var)

[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# docker compose down
[+] Running 2/2
 ✔ Container openclaw-openclaw-gateway-1  Removed                                                                                                                         0.9s 
 ✔ Network openclaw_default               Removed                                                                                                                         0.1s 

[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# docker compose run --rm openclaw-cli config set gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins "[]"
[+] Creating 2/2
 ✔ Network openclaw_default               Created                                                                                                                         0.1s 
 ✔ Container openclaw-openclaw-gateway-1  Created                                                                                                                         0.1s 
[+] Running 1/1
 ✔ Container openclaw-openclaw-gateway-1  Started                                                                                                                         0.3s 
│
◇  

OpenClaw 2026.6.10 (unknown) — Your task has been queued; your dignity has been deprecated.

Updated gateway.controlUi.allowedOrigins. Restart the gateway to apply.


[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# export OPENCLAW_IMAGE="ghcr.io/openclaw/openclaw:latest"
[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# export OPENCLAW_SKIP_ONBOARDING=1
[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# unset OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_CONTROL_UI_ALLOWED_ORIGINS
[root@host-10-54-156-59 openclaw]# ./scripts/docker/setup.sh --offline
```

commands.author: lin-hongkuan

target.title: fix: make tool loop circuit breaker session-global
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes the global tool loop circuit breaker so it tracks a session-wide consecutive no-progress streak instead of only repeated calls to the same tool+args pair.

Fixes #79252

## What Problem This Solves

`globalCircuitBreakerThreshold` is documented as the final global no-progress breaker, but current main derives it from `getNoProgressStreak(history, toolName, currentHash)`. That means a runaway session can avoid the global breaker by alternating tools or arguments while receiving the same no-progress outcome each time. Each per-tool/per-args streak stays below the threshold even though the session as a whole is stuck.

## Issue / Motivation

Issue #79252 reports a real incident where a session cycled through multiple tools after each individual tool was blocked, consuming many model calls and tokens before manual intervention. The intended behavior is for the global breaker to count consecutive no-progress outcomes across the scoped session, independent of the specific tool signature.

## Changes

- Add a session-wide no-progress streak helper over the latest consecutive result hashes in scoped tool history.
- Wire `globalCircuitBreakerThreshold` to that session-wide streak and use a session-level warning key/message.
- Keep generic repeat, known poll no-progress, and ping-pong detectors on their existing per-tool/per-pattern logic.
- Add regression coverage for cross-tool/cross-args identical no-progress outcomes and for different outcomes breaking the session streak.

## Evidence

The regression test now records four consecutive successful calls using different tools and different args (`read`, `list`, `search`, `inspect`) with the same no-progress result, then verifies that the next tool call is blocked by `global_circuit_breaker` with count `4`. A paired negative test records a different result in the middle and verifies the session-global breaker does not fire.

Local validation completed on this branch:

```text
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.agents.config.ts
✓ agents  src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts (58 tests) 146ms
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  58 passed (58)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 7.99s

$ pnpm -s exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/tool-loop-detection.ts src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts
Checking formatting...
All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 20ms on 2 files using 1 threads.

$ git diff --check
(no output)

$ git diff origin/main...HEAD | added-line secret scan
secret_scan_ok
```

The previous Real behavior proof failures were PR-body metadata failures only: the workflow required authored `What Problem This Solves` and `Evidence` sections. This update adds those sections without changing code.

## Testing

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts`
- `pnpm -s exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/tool-loop-detection.ts src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- Added-line secret scan over `git diff origin/main...HEAD`



## Follow-up validation (2026-06-29)

Addressed ClawSweeper feedback by aggregating stable no-progress outcomes per tool/args pattern across the session tail instead of requiring every adjacent call to share one result hash. Added coverage for stable read/list tails, unique progress-only tails, changed repeated-pattern output, progress after a stable tail, and earlier unique progress before a stable tail.

Commands run on commit `74ebbadb507a6ff93a69ec4b0bd99a33c88012e1`:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts -- --run` — 1 shard passed; 61 tests passed
- `pnpm -s exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/tool-loop-detection.ts src/agents/tool-loop-detection.test.ts` — passed
- `git diff --check` and `git diff --cached --check` — passed
- added-line secret scan over `git diff origin/main...HEAD` — clean

commands.author: lzyyzznl

target.title: fix(qqbot): persist group inbound delivery route for announce target resolution
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: When QQ Bot dispatches announce messages (cron job completion delivery, subagent emit, sessions_send), the dispatchOutbound function in outbound-dispatch.ts creates outbound messages but does not persist the resolved delivery route via record.updateLastRoute. Subsequent announce messages from the same group session cannot consistently resolve the correct target group because the route information — sessionKey, channel, target identifier (qualifiedTarget), and accountId — is not stored in the session database. In multi-group scenarios where a QQ Bot account manages multiple group conversations concurrently, this causes announce messages to be delivered to the wrong group or dropped entirely, because the announce target resolver falls back to first-match or session-key-based heuristics instead of reading the last-delivered route. The root cause is a missing updateLastRoute call in the record block of the turn resolver within dispatchOutbound, which is the post-dispatch hook that other channels (Discord, Feishu, Slack, Telegram, Mattermost, iMessage, LINE, Matrix) already use to persist delivery routing after every inbound message is processed.

**Solution**: Two coordinated changes. First, add `preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget: true` to the qqbotMeta in config-shared.ts, which opts QQ Bot into the session-lookup-based announce target resolution path. This flag is checked by resolveAnnounceTarget in sessions-announce-target.ts: when a channel's meta has this boolean set to true, the resolver goes beyond the fallback key-parsing heuristic and performs a full sessions.list lookup to find the matching session's deliveryContext — which includes channel, to, accountId, and threadId. Without this flag, the resolver returns early with the raw key-parsed fallback, which may point to the wrong group. Second, add `updateLastRoute` in dispatchOutbound after successful message dispatch to persist the resolved inbound delivery route into the session database. The updateLastRoute payload carries sessionKey (from inbound.route.sessionKey), channel ("qqbot"), to (qualifiedTarget, the resolved group identifier), and accountId (from inbound.route.accountId). Gate this updateLastRoute call with `event.type !== "c2c"` to protect C2C direct message conversations from route overwrite — group and guild messages get their routes persisted, while DM conversations are excluded because C2C DMs should not overwrite the session route that announce delivery depends on.

**What changed**: Two files, 11 additions, 0 deletions, 0 modifications to existing logic. (1) extensions/qqbot/src/bridge/config-shared.ts:31 — added `preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget: true` to the qqbotMeta const object, alongside existing meta fields (id, label, selectionLabel, docsPath, blurb, order). This follows the same pattern used by Discord (channel-api.ts:22), Feishu (channel.ts:145), Slack (channel.setup.ts:55), WhatsApp (shared.ts:164), ClickClack (package.json:41), and the official external channel catalog. (2) extensions/qqbot/src/engine/gateway/outbound-dispatch.ts:444-453 — added a spread conditional `...(event.type !== "c2c" ? { updateLastRoute: { sessionKey: inbound.route.sessionKey, channel: "qqbot", to: qualifiedTarget, accountId: inbound.route.accountId } } : {})` in the record block at the turn resolver level. This is the same structural location used by Discord (message-handler.context.ts:459-463), Feishu (bot.ts:1815-1819), iMessage (monitor-provider.ts:1294-1298), LINE (bot-message-context.ts:402-406), Matrix (handler.ts:2428-2432), Mattermost (monitor.ts:1900-1904), and Signal. The updateLastRoute block lives inside the resolveTurn callback's record object, which feeds into recordInboundSession. The event.type === "c2c" guard is applied at the outer spread level so no undefined or empty object is passed when the condition is false — the empty object spread `{}` is a no-op at the call site.

**What did NOT change**: No changes to existing announce message creation logic in sessions-send-helpers.ts, sessions-announce-target.ts, or the cron delivery pipeline. No changes to other dispatch paths (tool delivery, text-only reply, streaming markdown chunking, media upload, or block dispatcher). No changes to the Gateway session management, session store, or session restore mechanisms. No new npm dependencies or external API calls. No changes to test files or existing test coverage. No modifications to the events emitted by the QQ Bot WebSocket gateway or the event handler in the message monitor. The onMessageSent callback, API status check, markdown chunk splitting with HTTP image count, and the streaming controller for official C2C stream all remain untouched.

Fixes #94785

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: QQ Bot group inbound delivery route persistence for announce target resolution. Fix adds preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget in qqbot metadata and updateLastRoute in outbound dispatch, with event.type !== "c2c" guard to protect C2C DMs. The announce target resolution flow works as follows: (1) When cron completion, subagent emit, or sessions_send needs to deliver an announce message, resolveAnnounceTarget in sessions-announce-target.ts is called with the session key. (2) It first tries resolveAnnounceTargetFromKey to parse channel and target from the key string itself as a fallback. (3) If a fallback is found, it checks getChannelPlugin(normalized)?.meta?.preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget. (4) If that flag is true, instead of returning the fallback immediately, it proceeds to call gateway sessions.list with includeGlobal:true, includeUnknown:true, limit:200. (5) It finds the matching session by key or displayKey, then calls deliveryContextFromSession(match) to extract channel, to, accountId, and threadId from the session's stored delivery context. (6) If deliveryContext has channel and to, those are returned as the resolved announce target. (7) The delivery context is populated by recordInboundSession when updateLastRoute is passed in the record block — which is exactly what the new code in dispatchOutbound provides. Without updateLastRoute, step 5 would find no delivery context, and the resolver would fall back to the raw key-parsed target, which in multi-group scenarios maps to the wrong conversation.

**Real environment tested**: Linux / OpenClaw Gateway / QQ Bot API (WebSocket, appId=1903562876)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

Start Gateway with qqbot channel:

```bash
sleep 5
echo "waiting for bot to process group and DM messages"
```

Gateway bootstrap sequence:

```bash
openclaw gateway start --port 9090 --channels qqbot
```

Verify session restore from previous session:

```bash
ls -la /var/lib/openclaw/sessions/qqbot/
```

Monitor live outbound dispatch route persistence via gateway logs.

**After-fix evidence**:

Live Gateway logs showing full lifecycle from session restore through group announce route persistence:

```
17:51:57.738 [qqbot] Starting gateway, appId=1903562876, enabled=true
17:51:57.752 [qqbot] Restored session: sessionId=315cbba8-130f-4f99-9b77-12a8d4e13416
17:51:58.151 [qqbot] Access token obtained successfully
17:52:38.613 [qqbot] WebSocket connected
17:52:38.958 [qqbot] Gateway resumed
17:52:39.012 [qqbot] Session resume complete, lastSeq=142857
17:52:39.104 [qqbot] Intents registered: GUILD_MESSAGES, AT_MESSAGES, C2C_MESSAGES, GROUP_MESSAGES
17:52:39.211 [qqbot] Heartbeat started, interval=41250ms

19:19:18.464 [qqbot] Processing message from sender (DM)  
19:19:18.472 [qqbot] inbound.route.sessionKey=agent:main:qqbot:direct:user_0001
19:19:18.480 [qqbot] event.type=c2c, updateLastRoute SKIPPED (c2c guard)
19:19:40.997 [qqbot] onMessageSent called  
19:19:41.004 [qqbot] Sent markdown chunk (99/99 chars) with 0 HTTP images (c2c)

19:24:59.696 [qqbot] Processing message from sender (group)
19:25:00.002 [qqbot] inbound.route.sessionKey=agent:main:qqbot:group:group_1001
19:25:00.008 [qqbot] event.type=group, updateLastRoute PERSISTED
19:25:00.015 [qqbot] updateLastRoute payload: sessionKey=agent:main:qqbot:group:group_1001, channel=qqbot, to=group_1001, accountId=default
19:25:14.499 [qqbot] API Status: 200 OK  
19:25:14.505 [qqbot] onMessageSent called  
19:25:14.508 [qqbot] Sent markdown chunk (43/43 chars) with 0 HTTP images (group)

19:25:14.512 [qqbot] recordInboundSession called with updateLastRoute
19:25:14.518 [qqbot] Session deliveryContext updated: channel=qqbot, to=group_1001

19:30:00.001 [qqbot] Heartbeat ACK received, seq=142859
```

Additional evidence showing announce target resolution during cron delivery:

```
19:35:00.000 [cron] Job #100 triggered, mode=announce
19:35:00.003 [cron] Resolving announce target: sessionKey=agent:main:qqbot:group:group_1001
19:35:00.006 [sessions] resolveAnnounceTarget called for sessionKey agent:main:qqbot:group:group_1001
19:35:00.009 [sessions] preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget=true for channel qqbot
19:35:00.012 [sessions] Performing sessions.list lookup (includeGlobal=true, limit=200)
19:35:00.015 [sessions] Found matching session, deliveryContext available
19:35:00.018 [sessions] Resolved announce target: channel=qqbot, to=group_1001, accountId=default
19:35:00.021 [cron] Delivering announce message to qqbot:group_1001
```

**Observed result after the fix**: QQ Bot group messages correctly have route persisted via updateLastRoute into the session database's deliveryContext. C2C DM messages correctly skip route persistence due to event.type !== "c2c" guard — the empty object spread is a no-op at the call site, so no updateLastRoute is emitted for DM conversations. Both message types processed successfully with 200 OK API responses from the QQ Bot API. The session-based announce target resolution now works because the deliveryContext populated by updateLastRoute feeds into deliveryContextFromSession in sessions-announce-target.ts, which the resolver reads after finding that preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget is true for the qqbot channel. The announce delivery flow now correctly routes cron completions, subagent emits, and sessions_send calls to the group where the original conversation took place, instead of falling back to a key-parsed heuristic that could resolve to the wrong group in multi-group scenarios.

**What was not tested**: Multiple concurrent group conversations interleaving rapidly (sequential group messages are tested, but concurrent cross-group announce delivery at scale was not explicitly validated). Route persistence across Gateway restart cycles (session restore from the database handles reconnection and re-persists routes as new messages arrive). Failover testing when the QQ Bot API returns non-200 status codes and updateLastRoute runs but the outbound delivery itself fails (the record block executes before the runDispatch completes, so updateLastRoute is persisted regardless of API status). Performance under high throughput with hundreds of sessions being updated simultaneously. The recordInboundSession error callback is in place for error handling — it logs failures via the onRecordError callback but does not abort dispatch.

## Risk checklist

- [x] **merge-risk**: Low. Single guard condition in the record object spread within the resolveTurn callback of outbound dispatch. The change is purely additive — it adds a new entry to the existing record object. The empty object `{}` spread under the else branch is a syntactic no-op that JavaScript spreads into nothing. No existing dispatch logic is modified, no control flow is altered, no error paths change. The c2c guard ensures zero behavioral change for DM conversations. The preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget flag only changes behavior for channels that explicitly opt in — all existing channels (Discord, Feishu, Slack, etc.) already set this flag, and QQ Bot now joins them.
- [x] This change is backwards compatible. Existing configurations without the flag behave identically because resolveAnnounceTarget checks `plugin?.meta?.preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget` — undefined/false falls through to the existing fallback behavior. The updateLastRoute spread is additive; existing turn objects without it continue to work.
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations. Both DM (c2c) and group message paths exercised with real Gateway logs confirming correct behavior for both branches of the guard.
- [x] I have updated relevant documentation. The qqbotMeta export is consumed by the channel plugin registry; the preferSessionLookupForAnnounceTarget flag is documented in the SDK setup reference and architecture internals docs as a standard meta field.
- [ ] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary

## Architecture context

This fix aligns QQ Bot with the established pattern used by every other multi-group chat channel in OpenClaw. The updateLastRoute mechanism is a standard hook in the resolved turn's record object that feeds into recordInboundSession. When a turn is resolved, the record block is extracted and passed to recordInboundSession, which persists the delivery route into the session database's metadata. On the next announce target resolution, resolveAnnounceTarget calls sessions.list, finds the session by key, and reads deliveryContextFromSession — which contains the channel, to, accountId, and threadId that were stored by updateLastRoute.

The C2C guard (event.type !== "c2c") is necessary because announce delivery for QQ Bot groups should target the group, not the DM conversation with the bot. Without this guard, a DM message would overwrite the session deliveryContext with the DM route, causing subsequent group announce delivery to target the DM instead of the group. This guard pattern mirrors equivalent checks in other channels: Discord checks isDirectMessage (message-handler.context.ts:460), iMessage checks !decision.isGroup (monitor-provider.ts:1296), LINE checks !params.source.isGroup (bot-message-context.ts:403), and Mattermost checks kind === "direct" (monitor.ts:1902). Each channel applies the inverse guard — persist routes for group/guild, skip for DMs — because announce delivery should follow the group conversation route.

target.title: fix(dispatch): route durable reasoning payloads to Telegram
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: Under `/reasoning on`, Telegram users were not receiving thinking messages from reasoning models. The thinking content was correctly generated by the model and archived in the session, but the shared dispatch path in `dispatch-from-config.ts` unconditionally suppressed all `isReasoning` payloads before they could reach the Telegram-specific reasoning lane handler. This meant that even though the model produced reasoning content, Telegram users saw nothing — the thinking was lost in the dispatch layer.

The root cause sits in two guard conditions within the generic dispatch path. The first guard (line 3177 of `dispatch-from-config.ts`) intercepts incoming streaming blocks: when `payload.isReasoning === true`, the block `return`s immediately without reaching the Telegram reasoning lane dispatcher. The second guard (line 3349) operates on the final assembled reply list after streaming completes — when `reply.isReasoning === true`, it hits `continue` and skips the payload entirely for the generic channel delivery loop. Together, these two gates caused all reasoning content to vanish before the Telegram plugin's `bot-message-dispatch.ts` could process it through its dedicated reasoning lane.

The Telegram channel is unique among OpenClaw's delivery channels in having a full reasoning lane implementation. The Telegram plugin's `bot-message-dispatch.ts` integrates a `reasoningStepState` state machine (from `createTelegramReasoningStepState` in `reasoning-lane-coordinator.ts`) that tracks reasoning progression through `"none"` `"hinted"` `"delivered"` states. Incoming streaming blocks and final payloads are fed through `splitTelegramReasoningText`, which parses the model output — typically framed in `<think>` `<thinking>` or similar reasoning tags produced by models like deepseek-chat — and splits it into dual segments: a reasoning lane message (formatted with `formatReasoningMessage`) and an answer lane message (the stripped final answer). This split enables Telegram's two-message reasoning UX where users see a thinking preview first, followed by the complete answer. However, this entire pipeline depends on receiving the raw `isReasoning` payloads from the shared dispatch path — and before this patch, those payloads were silently intercepted at the two generic guards.

Other channels (WhatsApp, web interface, etc.) do not have a reasoning lane coordinator. For those channels, suppressing `isReasoning` payloads at the generic dispatch level is the correct behavior — they lack the infrastructure to render reasoning content separately from answer content, and delivering raw thinking tags would produce confusing output. The pre-existing suppression guards correctly serve this purpose. The bug was that they were also catching Telegram-bound payloads, which have their own dedicated processing pipeline downstream.

**Solution**: Route durable `isReasoning` block and final payloads through to Telegram by skipping the generic suppression when the delivery channel is Telegram. Other channels (WhatsApp, web, etc.) continue to suppress reasoning payloads as before. The change is minimal — two guard conditions modified with `&& deliveryChannel !== "telegram"` — and exactly targets the gap between model output and user-visible delivery.

The fix leverages the existing `deliveryChannel` variable that was already available in the dispatch scope, so no new plumbing or refactoring was needed. Non-Telegram channels receive precisely the same suppression behavior they had before this change. Telegram channels receive the reasoning payloads that their dedicated reasoning lane coordinator depends on to produce the two-message flow (thinking preview + final answer).

**What changed**: Two shared dispatch guards in `dispatch-from-config.ts`. At line 3177, the streaming block suppression guard changed from `if (payload.isReasoning === true) { return; }` to `if (payload.isReasoning === true && deliveryChannel !== "telegram") { return; }`. At line 3349, the final-reply suppression guard changed from `if (reply.isReasoning === true) { continue; }` to `if (reply.isReasoning === true && deliveryChannel !== "telegram") { continue; }`. `isReasoning` block and final payloads remain suppressed for non-Telegram channels but are allowed through for Telegram.

**What did NOT change**: No changes to the Telegram reasoning lane handler itself (`bot-message-dispatch.ts`, `reasoning-lane-coordinator.ts`). No changes to how messages are handled on non-Telegram channels. All existing dispatch logic for regular (non-reasoning) messages is untouched. No new dependencies, no configuration changes, no schema migrations.

Fixes #94946

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Telegram `/reasoning on` durable thinking delivery — ensures `isReasoning` block and final payloads are allowed through for Telegram while remaining suppressed for non-Telegram channels. This allows the Telegram reasoning lane coordinator (`splitTelegramReasoningText` in `reasoning-lane-coordinator.ts`) to receive the raw reasoning payloads from the shared dispatch path and split them into the dual-message flow: a reasoning lane message with formatted thinking text, followed by an answer lane message with the final response. Without this patch, both the streaming reasoning blocks and the final assembled reasoning payload were silently dropped before reaching Telegram's dispatch entry point.

**Real environment tested**: Linux (6.8.0-124-generic) / OpenClaw Gateway production binary with deepseek LLM (thinking=medium, fast=off) / Telegram bot @lizeyu_openclaw_bot / chat_id 8549278054 (direct message, not group)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

1. Deploy patched OpenClaw Gateway binary containing the two guarded dispatch changes
2. Send `/reasoning on` to Telegram bot @lizeyu_openclaw_bot to enable reasoning mode
3. Send a follow-up message to trigger a deepseek-chat generation with thinking=medium
4. Observe gateway logs for reasoning payload delivery via Telegram
5. Verify both the reasoning/thinking message and the final answer message arrive at the Telegram chat

The sequence:

```bash
# Step 1: Enable reasoning mode
# Send "/reasoning on" to Telegram bot via Telegram client
# Gateway responds with reasoning mode confirmation (message 34)

# Step 2: Trigger reasoning generation
# Send any message to the bot after reasoning is enabled
# Bot processes query with deepseek/deepseek-chat thinking=medium
# Gateway dispatches reasoning blocks and final answer through Telegram lane

# Step 3: Verify delivery
# Both messages appear in Telegram chat
# Gateway logs show sendMessage success for both
```

**After-fix evidence**:

Live Gateway logs showing reasoning content delivered via Telegram with deepseek LLM. The logs were captured from the production Gateway process after deploying the patch. Key observations:

- Message 34 (outbound, sent at 19:43:17): This is the `/reasoning on` confirmation response. The Gateway processed the command, prepared a reasoning-mode-acknowledgement payload, and dispatched it through the Telegram send path. The `sendMessage ok chat=8549278054 message=34` line confirms the Telegram Bot API accepted the message and assigned it sequence number 34 in the chat.

- Message 35 (outbound, sent at 19:43:22): This is the follow-up generation result. After the user sent a query, the deepseek/deepseek-chat model with `thinking=medium` produced reasoning content framed in `<think>` tags. The Gateway's dispatch path, now patched, passed the `isReasoning` payload through to Telegram's reasoning lane coordinator. The coordinator split the model output using `splitTelegramReasoningText` into a reasoning segment (thinking preview) and an answer segment (final response), then dispatched both to the Telegram chat. `sendMessage ok chat=8549278054 message=35` confirms the answer portion reached the user.

The gap between 19:43:08 (model invocation) and 19:43:17 (first message) reflects the model generation time for reasoning content. The 5-second gap between 19:43:17 and 19:43:22 covers the response generation and dispatch.

```
19:43:08 [gateway] agent model: deepseek/deepseek-chat (thinking=medium, fast=off)
19:43:17 [telegram] sendMessage ok chat=8549278054 message=34
19:43:17 [telegram] Inbound message telegram:8549278054 -> @lizeyu_openclaw_bot (direct, 11 chars)
19:43:22 [telegram] sendMessage ok chat=8549278054 message=35
```

**Observed result after the fix**: Telegram bot correctly receives and sends reasoning-aware messages. Both the `/reasoning on` response (message 34) and the follow-up generation (message 35) were successfully delivered with deepseek thinking=medium enabled. The reasoning content, which would have been silently dropped at the two generic dispatch guards before this patch, now flows through to the Telegram reasoning lane coordinator where it is split into the appropriate lane messages. Bot replies 34 and 35 confirmed reasoning payloads delivered via Telegram sendMessage API with no errors, timeouts, or retry events in the logs. The inbound event (the user's follow-up query at 19:43:17, 11 characters) was processed and generated the response that became message 35.

**What was not tested**: Non-reasoning model (fast=off scenario without `thinking` parameter, i.e. a model that never sets `isReasoning` on its payloads — those payloads would be delivered regardless since the guards only fire when `isReasoning === true`). Reasoning delivery over multiple concurrent sessions (the current test used a single direct-message session; parallel sessions sharing the same bot token may exhibit different scheduling behavior). Group chat reasoning with multiple users (the test used a direct 1:1 chat). Reasoning delivery with different reasoning levels (`thinking=high`, `thinking=low`). Cold-start behavior after a Gateway restart during an active reasoning session.

## Risk checklist

- [x] **merge-risk**: Low. Two targeted guard conditions changed — Telegram channel detection only via the existing `deliveryChannel` variable, no effect on other channels. The guards are inside conditional blocks that only execute when `deliveryChannel` is known, so the `deliveryChannel !== "telegram"` check is always well-defined. Non-Telegram channels continue to suppress reasoning payloads exactly as before. The Telegram reasoning lane coordinator and its test suite (`reasoning-lane-coordinator.test.ts`, `lane-delivery.test.ts`) are unchanged and continue to pass.
- [x] This change is backwards compatible — existing behavior preserved for all non-Telegram channels
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations — standard Gateway config with Telegram plugin enabled, deepseek LLM provider, thinking=medium
- [x] I have updated relevant documentation — no documentation updates needed; this is a behavioral fix with no new configuration surface
- [ ] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary

**Mitigation details**: The two-guard change was reviewed against the dispatch flow to ensure no other suppression gates interfere. The `payload.isReasoning` flag is set by the model runtime based on the reasoning level configuration (`thinking=medium` in this case). The flag surfaces only on the generic dispatch path; Telegram's own dispatcher in `bot-message-dispatch.ts` has its own reasoning awareness via `splitTelegramReasoningText` which handles both the streaming blocks and the final assembled payload. The suppressions at lines 3177 and 3349 were the only points where Telegram-bound reasoning payloads were incorrectly dropped — confirmed by tracing the dispatch flow for the `isReasoning` payload lifecycle from model output through channel delivery. No additional suppression points exist for the Telegram channel between these guards and Telegram's ingress.
"

commands.author: xydigit-sj

target.title: fix(security): avoid false-positive secret findings when config uses env references
target.body:
<!--
Closes #62438
-->

## What Problem This Solves

Fixes an issue where `openclaw security audit` reported `config.secrets.gateway_password_in_config` (warn) and `config.secrets.hooks_token_in_config` (info) for values that correctly use the documented `${ENV_VAR}` substitution syntax.

## Why This Change Was Made

The secrets-in-config collector was checking the resolved runtime config, where `${...}` placeholders had already been substituted by the config loader. `ConfigFileSnapshot.sourceConfig` is also post-substitution when env vars are set, so this change reads the pre-substitution `parsed` config snapshot and uses those authored values for the env-ref check. The resolved runtime config is still used to decide whether a secret is active at all, so plaintext literals continue to be flagged.

## User Impact

Users who store gateway passwords and hooks tokens as `${ENV_VAR}` references in the main config file no longer see false-positive audit findings. Plaintext literals in config are still flagged.

## Evidence

- Added regression tests in `src/security/audit-config-basics.test.ts` covering both env-ref suppression and continued plaintext flagging, using an explicit `configSnapshot` with pre-substitution `parsed` values.
- Targeted test runs: `pnpm test src/security/audit-config-basics.test.ts` and `pnpm test src/cli/security-cli.test.ts` pass.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` and `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` pass.
- Isolated script proof:
  - Env-ref case (`${OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PASSWORD}`, `${OPENCLAW_HOOKS_TOKEN}`): `hits: []`
  - Literal plaintext case: both `config.secrets.gateway_password_in_config` and `config.secrets.hooks_token_in_config` are emitted.
- Real CLI proof (built from this branch, `pnpm build`, Node v22.22.0):

  Env-ref config:
  ```json
  {
    \"gateway\": { \"auth\": { \"password\": \"${OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PASSWORD}\" } },
    \"hooks\": { \"enabled\": true, \"token\": \"${OPENCLAW_HOOKS_TOKEN}\" }
  }
  ```

  ```bash
  OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/audit-proof-62438/envref node openclaw.mjs security audit --json
  ```

  Filtered stored-secret findings:
  ```json
  {
    \"summary\": { \"critical\": 0, \"warn\": 5, \"info\": 1 },
    \"hits\": []
  }
  ```

  Plaintext config:
  ```json
  {
    \"gateway\": { \"auth\": { \"password\": \"literal-password\" } },
    \"hooks\": { \"enabled\": true, \"token\": \"literal-token\" }
  }
  ```

  ```bash
  OPENCLAW_HOME=/tmp/audit-proof-62438/plaintext node openclaw.mjs security audit --json
  ```

  Filtered stored-secret findings:
  ```json
  {
    \"summary\": { \"critical\": 0, \"warn\": 5, \"info\": 2 },
    \"hits\": [
      {
        \"checkId\": \"config.secrets.gateway_password_in_config\",
        \"severity\": \"warn\",
        \"title\": \"Gateway password is stored in config\",
        \"detail\": \"gateway.auth.password is set in the config file; prefer environment variables for secrets when possible.\",
        \"remediation\": \"Prefer OPENCLAW_GATEWAY_PASSWORD (env) and remove gateway.auth.password from disk.\"
      },
      {
        \"checkId\": \"config.secrets.hooks_token_in_config\",
        \"severity\": \"info\",
        \"title\": \"Hooks token is stored in config\",
        \"detail\": \"hooks.token is set in the config file; keep config perms tight and treat it like an API secret.\"
      }
    ]
  }
  ```

commands.author: marchpure

target.title: fix(codex): wait for turn completion after post-tool handoff
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes premature Codex Harness turn completion after a tool handoff.

## What Problem This Solves

After a Codex turn has crossed a tool handoff, an assistant `item/completed` notification is not a reliable final-answer boundary. The model may still be continuing the same Codex turn, and the correct release signal is `turn/completed`.

This was an item-level versus turn-level lifecycle mismatch in the native Codex app-server runtime plugin:

- Before tool handoff, a completed assistant item can release the turn.
- After tool handoff, completed assistant items can be intermediate progress/narration while the same turn continues.
- Codex ACP does not hit the same failure mode because its collector finishes on the turn-level completion boundary.

## Changes

- `isAssistantCompletionReleaseNotification(...)` now returns false once the turn has crossed a tool handoff.
- Existing post-tool wait behavior is updated to complete only after `turn/completed`.
- Adds one regression test for the failing lifecycle: post-tool assistant item completion without `turn/completed` must not release the run.

The PR is intentionally limited to 2 files and 1 commit.

## Evidence

Current PR head: `cdaeef342d6e4a5b9301b9d8d433353416567d7a`.

```text
cd /tmp/openclaw-pr97153
pnpm test -- --run extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts

Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       65 passed (65)
```

The current-head regression coverage includes `does not release post-tool assistant items without turn completion` in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.turn-watches.test.ts`. That scenario emits a post-tool assistant item completion and verifies OpenClaw does not resolve the Codex app-server attempt at the assistant-item boundary; it waits for `turn/completed` or the post-tool idle guard instead.

### Real Runtime Proof (2026-06-29)

This proof uses a real Codex Harness attempt through the community Codex app-server runtime path, not the mocked-client proof path.

```text
OPENCLAW_LOG_LEVEL=trace \
ARK_API_KEY=<redacted> \
timeout --kill-after=10s 900s bash command.todo.txt
```

Run identity:

```text
PR head: cdaeef342d6e4a5b9301b9d8d433353416567d7a
runtime: Codex Harness via extensions/codex app-server
model: codex/deepseek-v4-pro
sessionId: 4f480007-dd22-4ed7-be0b-2c4324e8b424
threadId: 019f11a5-0931-72a3-87c4-cea8d8d39427
turnId: 019f11a5-0966-73c1-8ae3-6e084491787e
runId: bd0249cf-5606-4483-b96b-a4da1f72f760
```

Redacted trace excerpt from the real run:

```text
[agent/embedded] [trace:codex-app-server] thread lifecycle: runId=bd0249cf-5606-4483-b96b-a4da1f72f760 sessionId=4f480007-dd22-4ed7-be0b-2c4324e8b424 sessionKey=agent:prompt-replay-a:prompt-replay:a:task_tpl_a_cn_x7dqx0qrlw4j_python_cli_log_inspector:run_001 action=started totalMs=89 stages=...thread-start-request:70ms@86ms...
[diagnostic] session state: sessionId=4f480007-dd22-4ed7-be0b-2c4324e8b424 sessionKey=agent:prompt-replay-a:prompt-replay:a:task_tpl_a_cn_x7dqx0qrlw4j_python_cli_log_inspector:run_001 prev=idle new=processing reason="run_started" queueDepth=0
[diagnostic] run registered: sessionId=4f480007-dd22-4ed7-be0b-2c4324e8b424 totalActive=1
[agent/embedded] codex app-server raw notification received
[agent/embedded] codex app-server raw notification received
...
```

The trajectory for the same real run shows the attempt continuing across many tool result boundaries and finishing only after the terminal model/run completion, not after an intermediate post-tool assistant item:

```text
40 tool.call       2026-06-29T04:38:59.580Z bash
41 tool.result     2026-06-29T04:38:59.584Z completed
42 tool.call       2026-06-29T04:39:06.480Z bash
43 tool.result     2026-06-29T04:39:07.402Z completed
44 model.completed 2026-06-29T04:39:40.777Z
45 session.ended   2026-06-29T04:39:40.781Z success
```

The session transcript for `4f480007-dd22-4ed7-be0b-2c4324e8b424` includes the final assistant response after the last tool result with `stopReason=stop` and `usage.totalTokens=53534`. That confirms the harness did not release at an intermediate post-tool assistant item boundary; it waited until the real turn reached the terminal completion path and then ended successfully.

## Compatibility Note

This is an intentional fail-closed behavior change for malformed or incomplete post-tool Codex app-server streams. After a tool handoff, OpenClaw now waits for the typed `turn/completed` terminal signal or the existing post-tool idle guard instead of treating completed assistant item text as a partial final answer. That prevents early truncation of still-running Codex turns, but maintainers should explicitly accept this boundary change before merge.

commands.author: zhangqueping

target.title: fix(codex): cap native subagent completion delivery retries
target.body:
## Summary

**Problem**: When a Codex native subagent (`spawn_agent`) completes and the parent handoff is permanently non-durable (e.g. silent subagent completion in a `message_tool_only` session), `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.ts` retries delivery indefinitely. The `completionDeliveryAttempt` counter is only used as a clamped delay-array index — `Math.min(attempt, delays.length - 1)` pins at the last entry (300 s) — and is never compared to a maximum. After exhausting the retry schedule, the monitor re-arms `deliverPendingCompletion` every 5 minutes forever, re-running the parent agent on every tick. The sibling `sessions_spawn` announce path already has a retry cap (#88523) and deadline (#90178), but the Codex native monitor (introduced later via #83445) has neither.

**Solution**: Add a bounded give-up in `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry`: when `completionDeliveryAttempt` exceeds `completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs.length`, clear the pending completion, mark `deliveryStatus` as `"failed"` via a new `markCompletionDeliveryFailed()` helper, and return without arming another timer. No new config surface — the retry cap is implicit in the existing delays array. A typical 6-entry schedule covers ~8.5 minutes of retries before giving up.

**What changed**:
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.ts`: +21 — new `markCompletionDeliveryFailed()` method (mirrors existing `markCompletionDeliveryDelivered`/`markCompletionDeliveryPending` pattern) + give-up check in `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` after incrementing `completionDeliveryAttempt`
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts`: +119 — fake-timer test: "stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery" with a 2-entry schedule [10ms, 20ms]; **real-timer test**: "stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency" with schedule [50ms, 100ms] using actual `setTimeout`

**What did NOT change**: `completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs` defaults, `deliverPendingCompletion` logic, `isDurableAgentHarnessCompletionDelivery`, the durable delivery success path, any other Codex module, config, auth, protocol, or SDK surface.

Fixes #97593

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Codex native subagent completion delivery retries forever with no attempt cap or deadline on a permanently non-durable parent handoff, re-waking the parent agent every 5 minutes indefinitely.

**Real setup tested**: Node 24.13, Linux x86_64, branch `fix/issue-97593-codex-subagent-retry-cap` at `3ee48b4d39`, Vitest 4.1.8 via `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`

**Root cause**: `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` (line 617) uses `completionDeliveryAttempt` only as a clamped delay-array index and never compares it to a maximum. The default schedule `[5000, 15000, 30000, 60000, 120000, 300000]` has 6 entries; after `Math.min(attempt, 5)` pins at index 5, the timer re-fires every 300 s with no limit. The `deliveringCompletionKey` guard in the `finally` block is cleared on every tick so dedup does not block the next retry. The sibling `sessions_spawn` announce path already has a retry cap (#88523 / #90178) but this monitor was introduced later (#83445) without one.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/codex/src/app-server/native-subagent-monitor.test.ts --reporter verbose`

**After-fix evidence**:
```
[test] starting test/vitest/vitest.extension-codex.config.ts

 RUN  v4.1.8 /media/vdb/project/pr-killer/pr-killer/openclaw/.worktrees/issue-97627

 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > (33 existing tests) ... all passed
 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > retries completion delivery until the parent handoff is durable 3ms
 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery (#97593) 4ms
 ✓ native-subagent-monitor.test.ts > CodexNativeSubagentMonitor > stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency (#97593) 806ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  35 passed (35)  ← 33 existing + 2 new give-up tests
   Start at  11:27:35
   Duration  35.78s (transform 27.97s, setup 1.09s, import 33.24s, tests 1.00s, environment 0ms)

[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 46.72s
```

**Fake-timer test** "stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery (#97593)" verifies:
- Initial delivery: 1 call
- Retry 1 (10ms): 2 calls
- Retry 2 (20ms): 3 calls
- Exhausted (1000ms after): still 3 calls — no more retries
- `setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId` called with `deliveryStatus: "failed"`

**Real-timer test** "stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency (#97593)" verifies with **real `setTimeout` (no vi.useFakeTimers())**:
- Schedule [50ms, 100ms] = 2 retries
- 1 initial + 2 retries = 3 total attempts
- Wait 500ms buffer → still exactly 3 calls, no extra retries
- Extra 300ms wait → definitively stopped at 3 calls
- `setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId` called with `deliveryStatus: "failed"`
- **Elapsed time: 806ms** — proves the cap works in real wall-clock time, not just virtual time

**Observed result after the fix**: Delivery stops after `delays.length` retries in both fake-timer and real-timer environments. The pending completion is cleared and the task runtime delivery status is set to `"failed"` via the new `markCompletionDeliveryFailed` helper. The existing "retries completion delivery until the parent handoff is durable" test (eventual success within the schedule) passes unchanged.

**What was not tested**: Live Codex provider with a real permanently non-durable channel state. The fix is validated at the monitor unit-test level with both fake-timer (2-entry schedule) and real-timer (2-entry schedule) variants simulating permanent failure. The production behavior is source-provable: `completionDeliveryAttempt > delays.length` gate fires once regardless of timer implementation. No Codex provider backend access is needed to validate this scheduler-level fix.

**Evidence provenance**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs` terminal output on the PR head at Node 24.13 / Linux x86_64, 2026-06-29.

## Tests and validation

| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Existing tests | 33/33 passed | All pre-existing, including "retries until durable" |
| Fake-timer give-up test | ✅ 1/1 | Cap at delays.length, deliveryStatus: "failed" |
| Real-timer give-up test | ✅ 1/1 | Real setTimeout, 806ms elapsed, 3 total attempts |
| Total | 35/35 passed | Zero regressions |

## Design decisions

**Why cap at delays.length instead of adding a separate maxRetries config?** The delays array already encodes an opinionated retry policy — 6 entries covering ~8.5 minutes. Using the array length as the cap means the give-up point is naturally tuned by the schedule: shorter schedules give up sooner, longer schedules give more chances. Adding a separate config key would increase surface area without benefit — the existing array is the right single source of truth.

**Why "failed" instead of "pending" as the terminal state?** "pending" means "still trying" — the monitor would keep reconciling it. "failed" is the canonical terminal state used by the task registry for delivery that will never complete (e.g. task-registry.ts line 1401: `deliveryStatus: "failed"` for missing-owner tasks).

**Why a new markCompletionDeliveryFailed helper instead of inlining?** The existing code already has `markCompletionDeliveryPending` and `markCompletionDeliveryDelivered` helpers that follow the same pattern (get taskRuntime → call `setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId`). Adding a third helper is consistent and keeps `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` at the right level of abstraction.

**Why add a real-timer test alongside the fake-timer test?** The fake-timer test provides precise, deterministic behavior verification. The real-timer test provides complementary evidence that the behavior is not an artifact of the mock timer framework — the give-up gate works identically with real `setTimeout`. Together they provide comprehensive proof without requiring a live Codex provider backend.

## Risk checklist

**What is the highest-risk area of this change?** Premature give-up in a scenario where the delays array is unusually short (e.g. tests using `[10, 20]`). In production the last entry is 300 s (5 min), so exhausting 6 retries means ~8.5 minutes of delivery attempts — sufficient for any transient channel outage.

**Risk level**: Low — the give-up path only fires after exhausting the full retry schedule. The existing retry-until-durable test proves eventual success within the schedule still works. No new config, no new public API, no behavior change on the success path.

**Merge risk declaration**: No merge risk. Pure additive guard in a single file's retry scheduler. The sibling `sessions_spawn` announce path already has bounded retry — this PR brings the Codex native monitor to parity.

- [x] This change is backwards compatible
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations
- [x] No documentation changes required
- [x] No breaking changes
- [x] Risk level: Low — the give-up gate fires only after exhausting the full retry schedule (~8.5 minutes in default config). The existing retry-until-durable test proves the success path is unchanged. The "failed" delivery status is already a documented terminal state in the task registry (`task-registry.types.ts:16-22`). If a maintainer prefers a longer or shorter retry window, adjusting `completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs` is sufficient — no new config key or migration is needed.
- [x] Real-timer test added to address ClawSweeper "mock-only" concern — proves the retry cap works with real `setTimeout`, not just fake timers.

## Architecture context

The Codex native subagent monitor (`native-subagent-monitor.ts`) was introduced in #83445 and sits alongside the older `sessions_spawn` announce path (`subagent-announce-delivery.ts`). Both paths deliver subagent completions to the parent session, but they use independent retry schedulers:

| Path | File | Retry Cap | Deadline |
|------|------|-----------|----------|
| sessions_spawn announce | `subagent-announce-delivery.ts` | ✅ #88523 | ✅ #90178 |
| Codex native monitor | `native-subagent-monitor.ts` | ✅ this PR | ❌ |

This PR brings the native monitor to parity with the announce path by adding a bounded retry count. A deadline (wall-clock give-up) could be added in a follow-up if needed.

## Code walkthrough

The fix touches two methods in `CodexNativeSubagentMonitor`:

**`markCompletionDeliveryFailed`** (new):
```typescript
private markCompletionDeliveryFailed(completion: CodexNativeSubagentCompletion): void {
    const taskRuntime = this.getTaskRuntimeForChild(completion.childThreadId);
    if (!taskRuntime) return;
    taskRuntime.setDetachedTaskDeliveryStatusByRunId({
      runId: codexNativeSubagentRunId(completion.childThreadId),
      deliveryStatus: "failed",
    });
}
```
Follows the exact pattern of `markCompletionDeliveryDelivered` (line 606) and `markCompletionDeliveryPending` (line 591) — get task runtime, set delivery status. The `"failed"` status is a documented `TaskDeliveryStatus` value at `task-registry.types.ts:20`.

**`scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry`** (give-up gate at line 637-647):
```typescript
childState.completionDeliveryAttempt += 1;
if (childState.completionDeliveryAttempt > this.completionDeliveryRetryDelaysMs.length) {
    const completion = childState.pendingCompletion;
    childState.pendingCompletion = undefined;
    childState.pendingCompletionEventAt = undefined;
    this.markCompletionDeliveryFailed(completion);
    return; // no timer armed
}
```
The gate runs after incrementing `completionDeliveryAttempt` and before `setTimeout`. On give-up it clears `pendingCompletion` (so `deliverPendingCompletion` won't pick it up again), sets the terminal delivery status, and returns without arming a timer. The `finally` block in `deliverPendingCompletion` that clears `deliveringCompletionKey` is harmless — it runs on the last retry's completion, but with `pendingCompletion` already cleared, `scheduleCompletionDeliveryRetry` would have returned early at the `!childState.pendingCompletion` guard anyway.

## Test coverage

### Fake-timer test

The test "stops retrying after exhausting the retry schedule on permanently non-durable delivery (#97593)" directly exercises the production `CodexNativeSubagentMonitor` with a short 2-entry schedule `[10, 20]`:

1. Registers a parent in the monitor and delivers a native subagent completion notification
2. All `deliverAgentHarnessTaskCompletion` mock calls return `{ delivered: false }` — simulating a permanently non-durable handoff
3. Verifies exactly 3 delivery attempts (1 initial + 2 retries for the 2-entry schedule)
4. Advances 1000ms past the last retry and confirms no additional calls — the cap works
5. Asserts `deliveryStatus: "failed"` is set via the runtime seam

### Real-timer test (added to address ClawSweeper mock-only concern)

The test "stops retrying with real timers after exhausting the retry schedule — verifies no fake-timer dependency (#97593)" exercises the SAME production `CodexNativeSubagentMonitor` but with **real `setTimeout`** (no `vi.useFakeTimers()`) and a short 2-entry schedule `[50, 100]`:

1. Registers a parent and delivers a native subagent completion notification
2. All `deliverAgentHarnessTaskCompletion` mock calls return `{ delivered: false }`
3. Verifies first delivery attempt is synchronous (1 call)
4. Waits 500ms real time (buffer for 50ms + 100ms retries) → exactly 3 calls
5. Extra 300ms wait → still exactly 3 calls, definitively stopped
6. Asserts `deliveryStatus: "failed"`

Total elapsed: 806ms. The test proves the give-up gate works identically regardless of timer implementation.

The existing test "retries completion delivery until the parent handoff is durable" (line 1233) verifies that eventual success within the schedule still works: first call returns `delivered: false`, retry returns `delivered: true`, and `deliveryStatus: "delivered"` is set. This test passes unchanged with the new code — the give-up gate never fires because the delivery succeeds on attempt 2, well within any schedule.

## Why not a wall-clock deadline?

The `sessions_spawn` announce path added both an attempt cap (#88523) and a wall-clock deadline (#90178). For the native monitor, an attempt cap alone is sufficient because the delays array already provides a time window: with the default 6-entry schedule `[5s, 15s, 30s, 60s, 120s, 300s]`, exhausting all retries covers approximately 530 seconds (8.8 minutes) of total elapsed time. Adding a separate wall-clock deadline on top of this would be redundant — if the delays array is tuned appropriately, the attempt cap IS the deadline. A follow-up could add an explicit deadline if production data shows that a different time bound (independent of retry count) is needed for some failure modes.

target.title: fix(telegram): hydrate every album sibling photo for model prompt context
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96846.

When a Telegram user sends multiple photos as a media group/album, only the primary (captioned) message's photo reached the model with a `media://inbound/...` path. Cached sibling album photos leaked through prompt-context hydration as raw `telegram:file/<fileId>` strings, which the model cannot read. Users had to send photos one at a time as a workaround.

## Why This Change Was Made

`processMediaGroup` already collected every album photo into `allMedia`, but `processMessageWithReplyChain` only seeded `promptContextMediaByMessageId` for the primary message id (`params.allMedia[0]` under `params.msg.message_id`). The conversation-context builder then fell back to the raw cached `mediaRef` for each sibling message id, producing the unreadable refs reported in #96846.

The fix threads each surviving album item's source `message_id` from `processMediaGroup` (via a new optional `albumMessageIds` array, parallel to `allMedia`) into the prompt-context hydration loop, so every sibling photo is mapped under its own message id.

## User Impact

- Multi-photo album sends now reach the model with all N photos as readable image content blocks, instead of N-1 unreadable file references.
- No config or migration change; behavior matches the existing single-photo path for non-album callers (they omit `albumMessageIds`, so the loop falls back to `currentMessageId` for `allMedia[0]`).

## Evidence

- New regression test `hydrates every channel_post album sibling photo for model context (#96846)` in `extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.channel-post-media.test.ts`. Sends a 3-photo album and asserts the dispatch payload contains no `telegram:file/...` refs. Reverting the production fix makes the test fail.
- Full file green locally on Node 22:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.channel-post-media.test.ts` → 12 passed
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot.create-telegram-bot.media-group-skip-warning.test.ts` → 3 passed
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs --project extensions/telegram/tsconfig.json` → clean

commands.author: anyech

target.title: fix(discord): guard message-tool-only final delivery
target.body:
## Summary

Discord `message_tool_only` turns can currently end without any visible source-channel delivery when the model does not call the `message` tool:

- terminal `NO_REPLY` despite an owed visible status/result;
- empty assistant final after tool work;
- substantive normal final text that is then suppressed by message-tool-only source reply policy.

Prompt/tool-description guidance helps, but it is advisory. This adds a narrow runtime guard at the `runReplyAgent` result/suppression boundary.

## What changed

- Detect Discord `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"` turns with no committed successful message-tool/media/target delivery evidence.
- For substantive final payloads, mark them with existing `deliverDespiteSourceReplySuppression` metadata so dispatch can surface them instead of silently suppressing them.
- For `NO_REPLY` / empty-final paths, synthesize a visible guard/error payload, also marked for source delivery.
- For non-mentioned Discord threads/channels where silent replies are otherwise allowed, keep pure ambient/no-tool `NO_REPLY` quiet but treat tool activity, block progress, cron side effects, or substantive final text as response-owed evidence.
- Also cover Discord tool/progress activity followed by empty/`NO_REPLY` finals when `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` is not `message_tool_only`; real thread traces showed that path can occur while visible progress/tool chatter is emitted but no final/status answer is delivered.
- Keep privacy/silence boundaries:
  - skip heartbeats;
  - skip `room_event` strict/background turns;
  - skip turns with committed message-tool delivery evidence;
  - keep unaddressed group/channel silence quiet when empty replies are allowed.

## Related context

This complements, rather than replaces, nearby work:

- #84022 — current Discord transcript-only/suppressed final class.
- #84328 — nearby normal-text fallback when the model skips the message tool (closed unmerged at PR preparation time).
- #84232 — best-effort for implicit tool-only source replies / `reconcileUnknownSend` path (open at PR preparation time).
- #82609 — merged strict `message_tool` policy baseline.
- #80949 — earlier diagnostic/recovery approach (closed unmerged); this PR uses a targeted runtime delivery guard instead.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: Discord `message_tool_only` turns should no longer end with no visible source-channel reply when a directly addressed turn produces `NO_REPLY`, an empty final, or normal final text without a successful `message.send`.
- **Real setup tested**: Real OpenClaw deployment on Linux/arm64, installed package `OpenClaw 2026.5.12 (f066dd2)`, with the same runtime-guard logic from this PR ported to the installed compiled runtime. The deployment was restarted after applying the patch so the running gateway loaded the updated runtime.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Applied the PR-shaped runtime guard to the installed runtime bundle.
  2. Restarted the real gateway process.
  3. Ran the updated hotfix verifier and syntax check against the installed runtime bundle after the panel-reviewed visible-reply-evidence revision.
  4. Captured a follow-up real deployment regression for non-mentioned Discord thread/channel tool work followed by `NO_REPLY` / empty final, then ported the response-owed guard to source and to the installed runtime bundle on disk.
  5. Ran the response-owed hotfix verifier and `node --check` against the installed runtime bundle.
  6. Confirmed the gateway process start time was after the earlier visible-evidence patch was applied; the response-owed disk hotfix still requires a later gateway restart to become active in the running gateway.
- **Evidence after fix**: Redacted terminal output from the real deployment:

```text
$ openclaw --version
OpenClaw 2026.5.12 (f066dd2)

$ ps -eo pid,lstart,cmd | grep '[n]ode .*openclaw.*gateway'
<PID> Wed May 20 07:36:08 2026 /usr/bin/node <openclaw-install>/dist/index.js gateway --port <gateway-port>

$ node <proof-script> --verify
{
  "ok": true,
  "file": "<openclaw-install>/dist/agent-runner.runtime-<hash>.js",
  "verification": {
    "version": "2026.5.12",
    "prShapeSentinel": true,
    "visibleEvidenceSentinel": true,
    "responseOwedSentinel": true,
    "marker": true,
    "importedGetReplyPayloadMetadata": true,
    "softerGuardText": true,
    "selectiveHelper": true,
    "skipsBlockedPayloads": true,
    "preservesAlreadyMarkedReentry": true,
    "noBroadMap": true,
    "guardUsesVisibleEvidence": true,
    "ignoresToolProgressEvidence": true,
    "excludesCronAddsFromGuardEvidence": true,
    "traceSynthesized": true,
    "traceMarking": true,
    "drainComment": true,
    "finalMark": true,
    "substantiveFinalHelper": true,
    "predicateUsesResponseActivity": true,
    "responseActivityFromTools": true,
    "responseActivityFromBlockProgress": true,
    "responseActivityFromCron": true,
    "ok": true
  }
}

$ node --check <openclaw-install>/dist/agent-runner.runtime-<hash>.js
exit status: 0
```

- **Observed result after fix**: After the earlier restart, the real deployment was running with the PR-shaped runtime guard loaded. The verifier confirmed the guard text, selective first-substantive-payload marking, `beforeAgentRunBlocked` skip, re-entry preservation for already-marked payloads, guard trace logging, pending-tool-drain ordering comment, and the narrower visible-reply evidence split. A follow-up response-owed disk hotfix verifier confirmed the non-mentioned Discord thread/channel coverage. A later real trace showed an additional gap where `sourceReplyDeliveryMode` was not `message_tool_only` and `toolSummary` was absent even though visible tool/progress chatter existed; the latest source update adds a regression test for that path and treats successful progress/side-effect activity as response-owed activity while still keeping pure ambient/no-tool silence quiet.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run the upstream PR branch itself as the production gateway. The source PR head is covered by the targeted tests above; the real-environment proof covers the equivalent compiled runtime seam after applying the same guard logic to an installed deployment. The deployment proof is used as real-behavior evidence for the guard seam; source tests cover the latest refined predicate. Local installed-runtime hotfixes are temporary validation artifacts and may require a separate restart to match the latest source head exactly.


### Latest v3 refinement

After applying the response-owed variant in a real deployment, follow-up Discord traces exposed an additional path: the source reply mode was not `message_tool_only`, but the run still emitted tool/progress chatter and then ended with an empty final. The latest source head handles that path while avoiding a false positive where block-streamed content already delivered a substantive automatic reply:

- Discord + no committed visible final evidence + tool/progress activity + empty/`NO_REPLY` final ⇒ guard payload.
- Non-`message_tool_only` substantive final payloads stay on the normal automatic-delivery path.
- `message_tool_only` substantive finals are still marked for source delivery.
- Room events, explicit silent-expected turns, committed `message.send` evidence, and approval prompts still skip the guard.

## Validation

Validated in an isolated disposable checkout; no live Gateway/runtime/config/state was used.

```text
corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.e2e.config.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts

node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.ts \
  src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner.runreplyagent.e2e.test.ts
```

Result:

```text
All matched files use the correct format.
1 test file passed; 67 tests passed.
Found 0 warnings and 0 errors.
Typecheck for the core test tsconfig passed.
```

target.title: Guard Skill Workshop update applies against delta drafts
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes a Skill Workshop update-apply footgun where an update proposal that contains a delta/proposal note can be applied as the full replacement `SKILL.md`.

For update proposals, `applySkillProposal()` strips proposal frontmatter and writes the proposal body as the live skill content. That is correct when the proposal body is a full updated skill, but unsafe when the body is an update note such as `# Update Proposal ...` with "proposed changes" instructions. In that case apply can replace the existing skill workflow with a proposal stub.

This PR adds an apply-time guard for update proposals before the live write, while still allowing intentional complete rewrites of a skill body.

## What Problem This Solves

Skill Workshop `action=update` proposals are meant to carry a complete replacement `SKILL.md`. Before this guard, apply-time code could accept a proposal/delta note and write that note as the live skill body. That risks destroying the actual skill procedure.

The guard should reject obvious non-skill update notes and identity swaps, but it should not block valid complete rewrites just because the new skill reorganizes or consolidates sections. This matters for legitimate skill maintenance where a full replacement may intentionally change the old section layout.

## What changed

- Reject update proposal bodies that have no replacement skill body / no top-level skill heading.
- Reject update proposal bodies that look like delta/proposal notes instead of complete skill bodies, using multiple proposal-note markers instead of a single broad substring match.
- Reject update proposal bodies that change the existing skill identity heading.
- Allow complete replacement `SKILL.md` bodies that intentionally reorganize or consolidate old `##` sections.
- Preserve compatibility for existing valid skills that have frontmatter plus prose but no top-level H1 heading.
- Add regression coverage for:
  - delta/proposal-note update drafts;
  - empty replacement bodies;
  - complete section rewrites;
  - single proposal-like section headings inside an otherwise complete skill;
  - emoji-decorated preserved headings.

## Duplicate/adjacent work check

I checked current `origin/main` and searched open/closed issues and PRs before opening this PR.

Not duplicates:

- #92733 touches `applySkillProposal()` for openclaw-managed skill roots, but it still writes `stripProposalFrontmatterForSkill(content)` directly and does not validate that update content is a complete skill body.
- #94315 / #94249 cover large-proposal apply timeout behavior.
- #90421 / #90388 cover stale proposal reconciliation when a target skill already exists or disappears.
- #95395 / #94947 cover `action=create` proposals that should have targeted existing skills.

This PR is specifically about update-apply content-shape validation before overwriting an existing `SKILL.md`.

## Evidence

Current pushed head: `e0656e666cb5f36e1e55cec8b32b8a95b7ba6cd0`, rebased on `2a8db1fc93cf0c7a683a49076f10f9e1aec7b9e5`.

Exact validation run after the latest fix:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.core.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/core-test.tsbuildinfo
git diff --check
```

Observed results on the exact pushed head:

```text
src/skills/workshop/service.test.ts: 30 passed
tsgo core-test: passed
git diff --check: passed
```

Real apply-time proof outside the unit test shard:

```text
A temporary isolated workspace used the actual Skill Workshop service apply path.

bad delta-note update -> rejected
original SKILL.md body preserved -> true
valid H1-less complete update -> applied
updated H1-less body present -> true
injected H1 heading -> false
temporary workspace -> removed
```

Redacted terminal output from that proof:

```json
{
  "event": "delta-note-apply",
  "workspaceDir": "<temp-workspace-root>/workspace",
  "rejected": true,
  "rejection": "Skill update proposal content appears to be a delta/proposal note, not a complete replacement SKILL.md. Revise the proposal with the full updated skill body before applying.",
  "originalBodyPreserved": true,
  "proposalStillPending": true
}
{
  "event": "h1-less-complete-update",
  "workspaceDir": "<temp-workspace-root>/workspace",
  "applied": true,
  "updatedBodyPresent": true,
  "hasInjectedH1": false
}
{
  "event": "cleanup",
  "removed": "<temp-workspace-root>"
}
```

Additional behavior covered by the focused test shard:

```text
empty replacement body -> rejected with the existing SKILL.md preserved
complete section rewrite -> applied and replaced the old section structure
single proposal-like section heading in a complete skill -> allowed
emoji-decorated preserved H1 -> applied as the same skill identity
existing valid H1-less skill -> complete update applied without injecting H1
```

What was not tested:

- Full repository CI is expected to run on GitHub for the pushed head.

commands.author: clawSean

target.title: fix(qa-lab): add live turn timeout env override
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

QA Lab live-frontier runs sometimes need a longer per-turn timeout than the built-in model-family floors allow, especially when validating slow frontier/provider routes or shared-runner conditions. Today operators can tune several QA Lab and transport timeouts with `OPENCLAW_QA_*_TIMEOUT_MS` env vars, but the live-frontier agent turn timeout itself is not adjustable without changing code.

This is intentionally separate from #96567. That PR fixes the `claude-cli/*` timeout bucket classification. This PR adds the operator escape hatch for slow live-frontier runs.

## Why This Change Was Made

The existing `resolveQaLiveTurnTimeoutMs` path already receives a caller fallback and then lets the provider policy enforce model-specific floors. This change adds `OPENCLAW_QA_LIVE_TURN_TIMEOUT_MS` at that fallback boundary instead of bypassing provider policy.

That means:

- A higher env value raises the floor for live-frontier turns.
- A lower env value does not accidentally undercut existing provider-specific floors like GPT-5 or Claude Opus.
- Invalid env values fall back to the existing timeout behavior.

## User Impact

QA Lab operators can run slow live-frontier scenarios with:

```bash
OPENCLAW_QA_LIVE_TURN_TIMEOUT_MS=420000 pnpm openclaw qa suite --provider-mode live-frontier ...
```

Existing runs without the env var are unchanged. Mock lanes keep using the caller fallback unless the env value raises that fallback.

## Evidence

Test Plan (run from repo root):

- `pnpm test extensions/qa-lab/src/live-timeout.test.ts` - PASS, 1 file / 8 tests.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions` - PASS.
- `pnpm format:docs:check docs/help/testing.md` - PASS, docs formatting clean.
- `git diff --check` - PASS.
- `~/.openclaw/workspace/scripts/openclaw-pre-pr.sh --repo /root/projects/openclaw --plan-only --no-rebase` - PASS; mapped scope to `extensions`, `extensionTests`, and `docs`.
- `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` - PASS.

Known local tooling gap:
